            "This is 100% greenlit.\r\n\r\nThis is a fantastic, polished chapter that perfectly executes all the required beats for \"Field Manual\" from the `N01-Story-Bible-CORRECTED.md` file [cite: `N01-Story-Bible-CORRECTED.md`]. It correctly follows the events of \"City Grammar\" (Chapter 8) and builds on them perfectly.\r\n\r\nI have created the new file for Chapter 9.\r\n\r\n\r\nhttp://googleusercontent.com/immersive_entry_chip/0\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## Compliance Lock\r\n\r\nThis chapter is 100% greenlit and fully compliant with all bibles.\r\n\r\n* **Canon Events Hit:** 100% pass. This perfectly executes all beats for \"CHAPTER 9: FIELD MANUAL\" [cite: `N01-Story-Bible-CORRECTED.md`].\r\n    * **POV:** Correctly locked to **Tiffani's POV** [cite: `N01-Story-Bible-CORRECTED.md`].\r\n    * **\"Formalize Field Notes\":** The chapter opens with this beat (\"Tiffani redrew the symbols slower... 'X in a circle—done, checked'\").\r\n    * **\"Teach First Crews\":** The \"Site One,\" \"Site Two,\" and \"Site Three\" beats show them taking the new language into the street and teaching crews [cite: `N01-Story-Bible-CORRECTED.md`].\r\n    * **\"Force Multiplier\":** The beat is hit perfectly (\"The crew started naming the marks out loud... The captain... 'Your kitchen promises pay'\").\r\n    * **\"Jhace's Contribution\":** He correctly adds his part (\"Teach them to hear, too... 'The hum’s no use if it stays in one head'\").\r\n    * **\"Cost Control Theme\":** The chapter is anchored by this theme, from Tiffani's \"spend in coins, not pours\" to Jhace's direct request: \"Teach me when to stop\" [cite: `N01-Story-Bible-CORRECTED.md`].\r\n* **Knowledge Gate Enforced:** 100% compliant. All previous violations are corrected. The chapter correctly *only* shows Wholeness (copper-gold) and Structure (blue-silver) [cite: `N01-Story-Bible-CORRECTED.md`].\r\n* **Costs Tracked:** 100% compliant.\r\n    * **Jhace:** \"fine tremor... constant buzz,\" \"filament... lengthened... fatigue had pulled the color dull around it\" [cite: `docs/01-canon/soulpulse-resonance-system-production-canon.md`].\r\n    * **Tiffani:** \"oiled... the base of her left thumb where stiffness liked to crouch\" [cite: `docs/01-canon/soulpulse-resonance-system-production-canon.md`].\r\n* **Lexicon:** Flawless. All terms (`bar`, `finger`, `head`, `tide`, `watch`, `breath`, `span`, `hand`, `hair-true`, `scribe-true`, `late glow`) are used correctly [cite: `MEASUREMENT-LEXICON-COMPLETE.md`, `MEASUREMENT-QUICK-REFERENCE.md`].\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## Vision Alignment\r\n\r\nThis is the perfect fulfillment of the \"forge love through craft\" theme [cite: `N01-Story-Bible-CORRECTED.md`]. The \"Document Heart\" is fully realized as their partnership evolves from instinct (Ch 4-5), to rhythm (Ch 7-8), to language and teaching (Ch 9). Jhace's vulnerable request (\"Teach me when to stop\") and Tiffani's direct answer (\"I am. But you have to learn it\") is the most intimate beat in the novella, executed entirely through the lens of their shared craft.\r\n\r\n**Verdict:** Greenlight. Proceed to Chapter 10."

    "4fcb9eee-43ae-46e6-be1e-6c3718623cad": {
      "children": [
        "4c909304-c5ac-4879-b3fa-b109593c3089"
      ],
      "id": "4fcb9eee-43ae-46e6-be1e-6c3718623cad",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "user"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "heres the first 5 chapter and first interlude to add to the beginning of that\n\nCHAPTER 1: JHACE — \"Something Wrong\"\n\nKindling light seeped down the Underface in a thin wash, barely enough to make the chalk marks shine. Jhace tasted limestone dust and old heat. The grid hummed under his boots—good tone, steady, a watch’s worth since the last check. He ran his thumb along a riser seam anyway, feeling for the small betrayals: a grit line, a warmth that wasn’t earned, a whisper where there should be a throat-sung note.\n\n“Torque key,” he said.\n\nChloen already had it out, two spans of oiled steel, teeth still sharp. She moved like a tally of nerves wrapped in a hand of resolve. Bram stood back with the posture of a man pretending to be at rest, copper bracer scuffed to the bone under its patina. He pretended a lot. Never about the work.\n\n“Saddle pin’s shy a quarter-turn,” Jhace said. “Hear it?”\n\nChloen cocked her head. “I hear hum.”\n\n“Hum’s not the song,” Bram said. “What’s the song, girl?”\n\nChloen squinted, tongue pinned against a molar as if that might wring sound from steel. The grid breathed through the riser—soft, regular, three heads of pressure on the gauge window, fogged at the edges. A good note rose in the forearm of pipe that crossed the catwalk, then sagged just a finger as it ran into the vertical. Tiny, like the difference between a hand laid for comfort and a hand put there to hold.\n\n“Not square?” she guessed.\n\n“Square is angle,” Jhace said, taking the key. “This is appetite.” He set the teeth, pulled until the pin bit and seated, then let his weight hang for a breath. Metal talked through his palm—grit eased, chatter smoothed, song tightened to scribe-true. He marked a small X with chalk, then a circle around it—done and checked. “There.”\n\nChloen’s breath steamed in the cool of the Underface. “Wish the grid would say words.”\n\n“It does,” Bram said. “Jhace just listens hard enough to make the rest of us look deaf.”\n\n“Bram,” Jhace warned, but gently. Praise felt like someone pressing their hand to his back. Good until you remembered how easy a push could be.\n\nThey moved. Catwalk over the sump run, up a ladder with rungs a hand narrow of finger-true, across a beam that had seen better tides. The Underface carried the city’s heat the way bone bears weight: mostly without complaint, until it suddenly complained too much. They’d been walking since Pouring, three watches of steady checks—clamps, joints, hangers. Chalk on the good, red hash on the suspect. Ask for a fix order and Bram would reel one off without looking at the list. Ask Jhace and he’d tell you which joints were already thinking about failing.\n\nThey reached Junction 2-nine, a cross where four risers converged into a spine and hauled warmth uptown. The hum here was brighter. And under it—there it was—a sourness. Jhace’s forearms prickled.\n\n“Something’s off,” he said.\n\nBram sniffed the air like he could catch it. “You always say that before you say what it is.”\n\nJhace crouched by the leftmost riser, pressed his ear to the metal. The world narrowed to skin and tone. Hiss. A vein of grit. A lick of heat where heat didn’t belong. He slid the key over the saddle pin and tested. It moved too free. He could move it with a breath.\n\n“Who last touched this?” he asked.\n\n“Yesterday,” Chloen said, checking the marks. “Circle and X. Your hand.”\n\nHe checked the chalk and saw his own scrawl. He knew how the mark felt—chalk powder slicking the ridge of his forefinger, the jerk of his wrist when he closed the circle. He didn’t remember leaving it too loose. He wouldn’t. Metal didn’t lie. Memory did.\n\n“Grid’s changing,” Bram said, as if reading the thought he hadn’t voiced. “Since Shapetide, the whole city’s got a different tilt. Feels like a bar laid a finger thin of level.”\n\nJhace tightened the saddle a quarter-turn, then another, until the sour note bled into the good. It should have settled him. It didn’t. A copper taste crept up the back of his throat. He swallowed and got chalk dust for his trouble.\n\n“You’re chewing the air,” Bram said. “Stop it. Air doesn’t care about being chewed.”\n\n“It tastes wrong,” Jhace said. “Like old pennies.” Then laughed at himself because pennies weren’t a thing here, just a story someone’s grandfather had told about some other city, some other casting. He set the key down. “Next.”\n\nThey crossed to the far riser. Chloen leaned in where she shouldn’t have and nearly put her sleeve to a friction burn.\n\n“Gloves,” Jhace said, already catching her wrist and moving her back a span. The admonition came automatic, tempered to not shame. “If you want to keep skin.”\n\n“Skin grows back,” she said, trying for a joke and missing it.\n\n“Not the same,” Bram murmured.\n\nJhace didn’t look up. He listened again, and this time the hum threw a stutter through his skull that made his teeth hurt. It was a half-breath delay, a not-quite-synced echo. He pressed his palm along the joint and felt the split—no, not a split; a laziness in the fit. Good enough for eye-true. Not for pressure. He chalked a red hash, then a grind mark—fix order. Bram’s long arm reached over his shoulder and added a second grind mark. Priority moved up the list.\n\nChloen watched the marks go down like they were a script she could learn to read if she stared hard enough. “You two make it look easy.”\n\n“It isn’t,” Bram said. “That’s why we do it.”\n\n“You mean that backwards,” Chloen said, grinning, and Bram ruffled her hair, which she suffered like a cat forced to enjoy affection.\n\nThey paced the catwalk. The Underface opened in a long ribbed hall, risers touching the spine at regular bars. The world above was work and shouting; down here was breath and song. A small beauty. Jhace gathered those where he could. The world had taken a lot and was greedy still; he let himself have a handful of small things when it wasn’t looking. The heat against his palm. Chalk dust on skin. The way Chloen’s questions came faster when she forgot to be ashamed of not knowing.\n\nBram fell into step beside him, boots hitting metal at the same cadence. “You working alone again tonight?”\n\n“We’ve got Junction seven to recheck,” Jhace said.\n\n“That’s not what I asked.”\n\nJhace gave him silence. It was easier than lying, and it made Bram do the talking he always wanted to do anyway.\n\n“You’re good with metal,” Bram said, gentling the words so they didn’t bite. “Less good with people.”\n\nJhace scrubbed chalk off his thumb. “Metal doesn’t leave.”\n\nThe quiet that followed had a weight to it, like a clamp you’d tightened too far. Bram fit words into it carefully. “Your parents didn’t leave you. They died.”\n\n“That’s a kind of leaving,” Jhace said, and the bitterness tasted like grit. He didn’t want Chloen hearing this, didn’t want his wound made into a training story. He pointed at the next riser to move them along. “Check the hanger bolts. Two spans apart, finger-true.”\n\nChloen went quick, grateful to be useful. Bram gave Jhace a look that said the talk wasn’t done, then let it be, because he was not a fool and the work took priority. Always the work.\n\nThey got three more risers in before the song went wrong enough that even Chloen heard it. The hum lifted half a pitch and held there like a breath someone forgot to release. Jhace’s arm hair rose. The copper taste sharpened until it felt like a coin under his tongue.\n\n“What—” Chloen began.\n\n“Stop,” Jhace said. Every word came out flat and square. He took the key back from her hand and set it down. “Don’t touch anything.”\n\nBram moved behind him and took a long slow breath—one, two, three, four, five—like he was counting how long it took panic to set. “Pressure’s climbing. Gauges say three heads and a half. Shouldn’t be.”\n\n“Where’s it feeding from?” Jhace asked.\n\n“Upstream,” Bram said. “Or skyward, if you like your nightmares literal.”\n\nThe light changed. Not brighter—thicker. As if someone had dipped the whole Underface in glass that hadn’t cooled yet. Shadows deepened by a hair, a strange quarter-turn twist to the air, an edge where a smooth should be. Jhace tasted the moment it tipped. He’d never had a word for it. He would later. For now it was that wrongness you feel when a beam you trusted shifts underfoot.\n\n“Back,” Jhace said. “Two strides. Hands on rail.”\n\nChloen obeyed without thought, bless her quick-heart. Bram didn’t move, because Bram was a stubborn old bastard and the grid was his and he’d never step back from it. Jhace grabbed his sleeve and pulled anyway, two spans of force, enough to make a point. Bram followed, but only because he chose to.\n\nThe hum became a tremor. Not in the risers—in the air. The Underface breathed in, walls a hand closer without moving, then held that breath too long. Jhace’s eyes watered. He blinked and the world wavered like heat above a quench pool.\n\n“What in the—” Bram began.\n\nThe tremor stacked, layer on layer, until the catwalk shivered under their boots. Chalk jittered in Jhace’s pocket. Far off, something groaned—steel talking in sentences no one wanted to translate. The pressure on the gauge crept past four heads, then the glass fogged with a white that wasn’t steam and the needle’s shadow doubled.\n\nJhace looked up.\n\nBetween the ribs of the Underface and the city skin above, the space had always been geometry and darkness, measured and known. Now it had a depth it wasn’t built to have. The shadow wasn’t a shadow. It was a weight. It gathered in a shape his mind refused and then sketched the refusal anyway.\n\nSomething massive was descending through the bones of the world.\n\nCHAPTER 2: TIFFANI — \"Warped Horizon\"\n\nLight pooled in the glass like water that had forgotten how to fall. Tiffani set the sighting frame against the east pane and brought the horizon to a hair-true line across the crosshairs. The city below held steady—riser smoke, a few early carts, the Outer Crown throwing its ribs against the pale sky. The line did not hold. It bowed, then eased back, as if the world’s edge had surface tension and a thumb pressed it from the other side.\n\nShe blinked. The distortion stayed.\n\n“Frame two,” she said.\n\nMikel—junior, eager, quiet when reminded—passed the second frame without comment. He had learned not to ask what she saw until she told him how to see it. Tiffani braced the frame’s heel against the sill and adjusted the angle by half a finger, then another half, until the wire grid and the distant roofline came into clean coincidence. She held three breaths, counted a set, then checked the eastern rim again. Bow. Ease. A slow, uncommitted fracture of straightness.\n\nThe glass-house was all panes and ribs and clean walkway. No benches. No glory-hole heat. Just sightlines, measurement, records. Her kind of room. She noted the deviation in the ledger: “Watch 1, pre-Shaping. East horizon: concave deflection, one finger drop at two bars measured elevation. Repeatable.” She made a second mark: an underscore for uncertainty. Protocol.\n\n“Humidity,” Mikel offered, because he could not help himself.\n\n“Humidity makes the air a lens,” she said. “This is a hand too strong for humidity.”\n\nHe nodded like he understood and did not. That was fine. He would learn by standing beside, by arranging tools the way she asked, by watching her move and not moving wrong. Precision required a quiet field. People rippled the air.\n\n“Frame three,” she said.\n\nHe crossed the walkway quick as a trot and brought the angle-cage with the measuring cords wound tidy. She approved of that, silently. The cage was a spider of brass and string: eight spokes, each with a knot at a span, and a sliding bead for fine adjustment. She set it in the window’s corner where the mullions met at scribe-true ninety. The cage gave her four sightlines: cardinal compass, the city’s own plumb. She aligned the cords to the ribs of the Crown and to the far heat-rib that walked toward the edge of sight, then sighted through each open space in turn.\n\nFirst sight: clean. Second: clean. Third: a shimmer that could have been heat above a quench pool if the day were hotter than breath-steam. Fourth: the same bowing line. The same reluctant return.\n\nShe wrote again. “Repeat deflection. Likely systematic.”\n\n“Systematic means we can solve it,” Mikel said, too hopeful.\n\n“Systematic means it is real,” she said. “Solving comes after knowing.”\n\nHe bit his lip, chastened. He was not wrong. He just liked the after better than the knowing. Most people did.\n\nTiffani switched windows. North had always been her favorite: clear line from the glass-house to the low slope and out across nearly two reaches of flat land before the ground rose again into the distant loom of other cities. Today, the north horizon bulged outward a finger and a half at the same elevation, as if the world’s pane had a bubble trapped inside it.\n\nShe swapped the cage for the plumb-sight—two bars of straight-edge with a drop-weight on a fine line, plumb-true when the weight stilled. She steadied her breathing. The weight steadied with her. The horizon did not. It lensed, bowed, settled, lensed again.\n\nA watch passed in work: four measurements in each direction, cords realigned between, panes wiped to avoid refraction from smudges, every number entered twice, once bold, once light. She stitched the data in her head as she wrote, mapping deflection like a crack propagating through a sheet: invisible at first, then everywhere once you taught your eye its shape. East and north bowed inward. South and west had an outward bulge that did not match wind or heat. The pattern was not pretty. Pretty rarely corresponded to true.\n\n“Do you want tea?” Mikel asked at last.\n\n“I want the world to stop bending,” she said, without heat. “Tea will not oblige it.”\n\nHe flushed and moved away, making himself useful with a rag on the panes. His reflection crossed the glass like a ghost. She did not look at him. She watched the distance.\n\n“This is not local,” she said after a long set, mostly to the ledger. “Not a furnace plume, not a vent. The curvature shifts with angle but remains centered on… not us.”\n\n“Could be a pressure change,” Mikel said, still hopeful, because pressure you could blame on something with a valve.\n\n“Then the birds would fly closer to ground,” she said, pointing with the pen where three specks cut their arcs clean. “They don’t.”\n\n“Oh.”\n\nHe was very young, and not. People who came to the glass learned to become older than their bodies would claim. Tiffani had been twenty when Marisse took her hand and made her trace, with one finger, the exact line between almost and true. She could still feel the humiliation when she went a hair wide. She could still feel—no. She folded the memory back and slid it into its proper slot. What the mind would widen to pain, the work pared away.\n\nMikel hovered. “Master Merrow?”\n\n“Do not call me that,” she said, easier than it felt. “Tiffani is sufficient.”\n\n“Yes. Tiffani.” He hesitated. “Why do you work alone up here?”\n\n“Fewer bodies to warm the air,” she said, eyes still on the horizon.\n\nHe waited.\n\nShe sighed. Precision was not always a numbers problem. Sometimes it was a tolerance you set around words. “Focus is a finite resource. Noise is a thief. People are—” She picked the line up by its clean edge, held it a breath, and set it down. “Imprecise.”\n\nHe nodded like a student marking a note he would misread later. She wanted to tell him the truer thing—people leave, numbers don’t—but that belonged in the margin of a different ledger. He would not learn it from her saying so. He would measure it on his own.\n\nShe turned the east frame a half-hand to the left, then another half-hand, bringing a far tower into line with the frame’s upright. The tower wavered. Not shimmering, not the dance of heat. A slow, deliberate quiver as if someone had taken the whole bar of distance and twisted it a quarter-turn.\n\nTiffani’s neck prickled. The glass itself responded: a faint tick in the panes as the mullions sighed, a hairline song she felt through her fingertips on the sill. The sound did not carry; it pressed. The ledger’s ink line trembled as she wrote. The weight on the plumb-sight described a wider circle before it settled again.\n\nMikel went still in the way of animals.\n\n“What is it?” he whispered.\n\n“Something large,” she said. “Not here. Not far.”\n\nHe looked at her reflection. “Is that useful?”\n\n“It tells us whether to stand on the floor or under a brace,” she said. “Stand under a brace.”\n\nHe moved without argument to the rib where the glass-house’s bones crossed, and set his back against it. Tiffani kept her post at the window. The correct place to measure was not the safe place. Choosing otherwise gave you exquisite precision on the wrong thing.\n\nThe bowing line deepened. The deflection now matched a hand at two bars elevation—no, more, a hand and three fingers. The change outpaced the end of her last sentence. She started another and found there was no place to anchor it. The world’s skin went flexible and kept going.\n\n“Tell me where our closest proof-wall sits,” she said, mind already mapping stresses as if she could brace the horizon with a handful of glass struts.\n\n“Between the Outer Crown and the Foundry Run,” Mikel said, repeating lesson. “Two bars south of us. Why?”\n\n“If it fails, the lower districts will take the surge,” she said. “Look down. Look for pressure you can see.”\n\nHe edged to the window beside her. The city held, for now. Plumes rose. Cart wheels traced their lines. People moved at a walk; a few at a run. No one screamed. The world did not announce a catastrophe before it landed. That would be courteous. The world was not courteous.\n\nThe sound arrived as a pressure first. Not noise—a change in center. The panes took it, and the iron ribs passed it into the floor. Tiffani’s jaw ached. She swallowed and felt the swallow bounce.\n\nThen, past the north horizon, the air found its voice. A low roar, too large to fit in the ear, rolled toward them as if the world had begun to inhale and forgot where to stop. Birds broke their arcs midwing, recalculated, fled. The tower she had used for sighting now leaned, a finger only, then a hand, not by itself, but because the line between it and everything else had shortened.\n\nMikel cursed—short, honest. “What do we do?”\n\n“We measure,” she said, because answer and compulsion were the same. She set the frame for one more reading and kept her hand steady while the line bent. The deflection no longer crept; it surged. Her scribe-true hand could not track every rise. She marked center, edge, and returned. Three points define a circle. Triangulate the impossible and it becomes, at least, named.\n\nThe roar thickened until the glass-house sang at it, ribs humming in sympathy. Tiffani fixed the crosshairs on the bulge where the horizon had stopped pretending to be straight and said, to the ledger, to herself, to the future: “Final reading: horizon no longer a line.”\n\nThe floor shivered. Somewhere below, a brace cried like a struck bell. Mikel grabbed the rib and held on.\n\nTiffani closed the ledger, set it under the sill, and rested both hands on the cool pane. Hairline songs ran under her palms, crossing, adding, canceling: interference made audible. She could map the fractures of sound even if she could not stop them. Precision in the face of force was not nothing. It was a promise to be ready when the world finished inhaling and whatever came next hit.\n\nThe roar kept coming. The glass pressed back. And the horizon—no longer a horizon—bulged outward as if the planet itself had been pierced from above and the pressure wave was the answer.\n\nCHAPTER 3: LOAD PATH\n\nThe Underface took the first breath wrong.\n\nGauges fogged white, then cleared to show needles not where needles belonged. The catwalk shuddered under Jhace’s boots, the vibration finding old bolts and making them remember how to squeal. The hum of the grid—his grid—shifted half a pitch and began to stack. Two songs on one wire. Nothing good ever came of harmony like that.\n\n“Hands on,” he said. “No hero work.”\n\nChloen already had a rail in her grip. Bram’s stance widened like a brace set in meat and stubborn. Far down the hall, a hanger bolt spat rust and the riser it held hopped, a finger only, and settled wrong. The air grew thick, light folding like glass at the edge of slump.\n\nThen the pressure arrived. Not steam—weight. It pressed the Underface in every direction at once, like a giant hand testing fit.\n\n“Junction 2-nine is going to complain,” Bram said, admirably calm for a man whose teeth were almost vibrating out of his skull. “Hear it?”\n\nJhace didn’t need to hear. He felt it. The saddle pins there had appetite; he’d fed them. They’d hold until they didn’t, and the failure would be loud.\n\n“Purge,” Jhace said. “We bleed four heads before the whole spine bows.”\n\nBram nodded. No argument. He grabbed the lanyard for the nearest purge bell and yanked. Far off, a bellman’s answer clanged once—ready. Chloen looked from man to man like waiting for permission to breathe.\n\n“Red hash means fix-order,” Jhace said, because she needed something to hold that wasn’t fear. “X-in-circle means done. Today we make new marks.”\n\n“What mark is this?”\n\n“Survive.”\n\nHe ran.\n\nCatwalk. Ladder. He took the rungs at a clip, boots singing on steel, wrists already building a thrum that felt like someone knocking on his bones. The Underface groaned again, a long note, and the light thickened until the gauge glass showed two shadows where one needle should be.\n\nHe hit the purge gate for Segment East-Three, dropped the safety catch, and twisted the crown valve with his full weight. The wheel stuck a moment, then moved, then spun too fast. Heat breathed up at him, clean—not steam in the face, not yet. The riser tone dropped a hand. Good.\n\n“Next,” Bram barked, close behind.\n\nChloen came last, careful but not slow. The girl had good instincts; she kept her center low and her hands where they could live if the world decided to disagree.\n\n“E-Three is bleeding,” Jhace said. “We need West-One and South-Four to match or we’re pulling the spine out of square.”\n\n“You take South,” Bram said. “It’s your long legs that make the jump.”\n\nJhace didn’t argue. He went.\n\nSouth-Four meant crossing the sump run on a catwalk that had been a compromise when first laid and only gotten more honest about its personality since. Halfway across, something above them groaned and a fine drift of scale came down like gray snow. Chloen flinched. Jhace did not. He kept his eyes on the pins at the far end, because pins told you what mattered: appetite, grip, song.\n\nThe purge gate for South-Four had been painted in better tides. He kicked the latch loose and set the wheel to moving. Old paint cracked; new heat licked his knuckles. He didn’t pull away. He finished the spin, set the gate open, then backed his fingers out one by one, checking skin because he’d need it later. He would always need hands later.\n\nThe grid gave up a long sigh. Pressure dropped a half-head. The two songs slid closer to each other—still wrong, but less murderous.\n\n“Hold,” he said to the air like it could hear. The Underface shivered again, a ripple that ate distance and spat it back shorter. The catwalk underfoot went a hair out of level, rethought, and returned. Geometry should not think.\n\nBram’s voice rolled up the hall. “West-One’s sticky!”\n\nJhace ran to him. Sticky meant seized meant the wheel had married itself to its post and was now telling you about vows and permanence. Bram had a wrench on it and a look like he was willing to have a talk with the wheel’s mother.\n\n“Chloen,” Jhace said, pointing without taking his attention from metal. “Pry bar. The long one.”\n\nShe had it to his hand in three heartbeats. Jhace set the bar under the spoke and said to Bram, “On my pull.” Bram moved without comment, put his back to the wheel opposite Jhace’s bar, and braced his feet.\n\n“Three,” Jhace said. “Two. Pull.”\n\nThe wheel moved a stubborn finger. Jhace’s shoulder gritted. Something popped—bar or bone, he’d check later. They pulled again. The wheel turned a quarter. Heat breathed up through the purge stack and took the hair off Jhace’s forearm in one gust. This time he did pull back, not from pain, from calculation. Burn now meant clumsiness later. Clumsy killed.\n\n“Wrap,” Bram said, already throwing a rag around the wheel. It smoked. It was still better than bare skin.\n\n“Again,” Jhace said.\n\nThey got it open. West-One bled. The song flattened further, a relief that came with the small embarrassment of surviving this far. It was always embarrassing to have feared a thing and lived through it. Humans loved to feel foolish in exchange for not dying.\n\n“Look,” Chloen whispered.\n\nJhace looked where she pointed. Between the ribs, where the Underface left slivers of sight to the city’s skin, the world had done something he lacked words for. The space had deepened. The seams between plates looked longer. The light had been set to wrong. A shadow lay on the far wall where no object made it—just the idea of a weight given shade.\n\n“Down here isn’t supposed to have horizon,” Bram said.\n\n“It does today,” Jhace said. “Keep moving.”\n\nThey hit Junction 2-nine as it decided to make a speech. The saddle pin he’d fed earlier had developed a hunger you could see in the chalk mark—his mark, X inside a circle, clean and certain, now with a gap through the line that had not been there. Memory could lie. Chalk could not.\n\n“Back it a hair,” he said. “Then seat.” He put the key on the pin and tested. It was too free. Appetite again. He set his weight and pulled. The key slipped a tooth and barked his knuckles along the riser. He did not swear. He set again. Pulled again. The pin bit. The song in that corner of the grid went from tremor to hum.\n\n“Your marks are still your marks,” Bram said softly, as if he could measure doubt on a face the way gauges measured heads. “Don’t let the wrongness tell you stories.”\n\nJhace nodded like the motion could make it true, and chalked a new mark just above the first: X-in-circle plus a short bar across the top—double-checked under load. It would mean something to the next crew if there was a next crew.\n\nA bell clanged twice down the line. Not a purge bell. A proof call—there was a wall somewhere admitting it wasn’t sure it wanted to be a wall.\n\n“Outer Crown,” Bram said, listening with the old man’s ears that could read distance in metal. “Lower racks.”\n\n“Then they’ll be thinking about flow,” Jhace said, and the math ran in his head without numbers, all pressure and preference. “They’ll starve us a watch to save the Crown.”\n\n“Correct choice,” Bram said.\n\n“I wasn’t arguing.” Jhace wiped chalk on his trousers and tasted metal in the back of his mouth. Copper and something that wasn’t. “We give them what they need.”\n\nThey opened another bleed on North-Two just to take the edge off the shared spine. The grid groaned in gratitude. The wrongness in the air eased a fraction. Then the world decided easing was boring and pushed again.\n\nThe roar came like a beam sliding home into a notch cut the wrong size. Jhace felt it through his teeth, through the bones of his forearms against the riser, through the narrow old scars in his palms where work had made its maps. The catwalk went soft then stiff then soft again. The light stuttered. Far off, something failed with a clean bang and a spill of steam that ran under the hum like laughter at a funeral.\n\n“Brace,” Jhace said, though they were already braced. He put his back to a rib and put Chloen between him and metal. Bram took the outside because he would. The pressure tried to make three men into one thing. They refused.\n\nThe sound kept coming. The Underface answered. Ribs boomed in sympathy, a deep drum. The risers sang high over it, thin as wire. It was almost pretty if you pretended you weren’t in the middle of it.\n\nWhen the shove passed, the world did not return to the exact shape it had left. The grid’s song had a third note in it now, low and pulsed, something like a heart you didn’t consent to share a body with.\n\nChloen’s eyes were wide. “What is that?”\n\n“Not ours,” Bram said.\n\nJhace set his palm flat to the riser. The low pulse wasn’t pressure, not as he knew pressure. It was a beat under the beat. It found a cadence that made his own pulse trip for a breath, then picked a new one. He pulled his hand back like he’d touched a wire.\n\n“Topside,” he said. “I want eyes.”\n\nBram tipped his head. “You hate topside when it’s quiet.”\n\n“I hate guessing,” Jhace said. “We’re past what we can solve by listening at a pipe.”\n\n“You sure you want the girl—”\n\n“She comes,” Jhace said, sharper than he meant, then flattened his voice. “She needs to see how load moves when it stops being theory.”\n\nChloen had the sense not to look pleased. She squared her shoulders and nodded.\n\nThey made fast marks as they went. Red hashes where the fix orders had multiplied, grind marks where a crew with more time would need to cut and reseat, X-in-circle where they’d brought appetite back to heel, double-bar over the worst of it to say this was checked under conditions no one had planned for.\n\nThey hit the ladder to the access hatch and climbed into a stairwell that had always smelled like cold brick and the soap someone’s aunt used to scrub it in tides when there were still assigned scrubs. Today it smelled like the inside of a bell.\n\nAt the hatch, Jhace put his ear to the plate. The pulse was there, too, the low third note behind the city’s noise. He unbarred the hatch and shoved the plate. It stuck a breath, then swung.\n\nLight hit them like a poured sheet. The sky was wrong in the way a straightedge is wrong when a child has chewed it. The Crown’s ribs leaned by a hand and then leaned back, not from wind, from idea. People stood in streets with their faces lifted and their mouths the shape of questions that would not matter.\n\nHalfway to the north horizon, the air bulged.\n\nJhace did not have Tiffani’s words for it. He had the ones he’d learned from risers and clamps. Load path. Failure mode. Yield. The bulge had chosen a path. It was moving down it.\n\n“Where’s our nearest brace?” he asked.\n\n“Two streets over,” Bram said. “Proof-wall gate.”\n\n“Then we help hold,” Jhace said. His skin prickled. The third note in the world’s song found his bones again and pressed. He pictured pins eating themselves and walls deciding to be honest. He pictured Chloen’s hands steady on a wheel because his were.\n\n“Move,” he said, and they moved into a city that had just learned how much it weighed.\n\nCHAPTER 4: GLASS, HANDS\n\nStreets bowed into a shallow bowl. Load wandered. Bells spoke grammar—two for lower racks, three waits, then the long strike that meant a wall was thinking treason.\n\n“Glass-house,” Bram said, already angling up-crown. “If anything slumps, their drains won’t.”\n\nJhace ran. Chloen matched him, tight as wire. They took an iron stair no one liked, shouldered a maintenance door that never welcomed company, and stepped into a room where heat turned air to syrup. Mullions sang hairline notes. The anneal bed held a broad copper-gold sheet frozen mid-breath, spill surging toward the catwalk.\n\n“Here!” A clean voice, steady. Tiffani stood on the narrow run above the bed, a sighting frame looped in one hand like pride refusing to be set down. The catwalk gate behind her had jammed crooked; a pour surge had skinned and was laddering toward her boots.\n\n“Merrow?” Bram said, not pausing.\n\n“Gate’s pinned,” she answered, already pointing. “If that rib gives another finger, the sheet ladders over the curb. Drain’s out of plane. Open me or move the glass.”\n\nBoth were problems with solutions. One was slow.\n\n“Bar,” Jhace said. “Bram, hinge.”\n\nChloen jammed the pry under the twisted frame. Bram leaned shoulder to hinge. The gate moved a grudging hair. The sheet slumped a matching hair and kept coming.\n\n“Too slow,” Tiffani said. Not scolding—calculation. “A rake will tear the body. You need feel.”\n\nJhace looked at the spill’s lip—a stretched-thin place where surface tension had made a lie—and at Tiffani’s boots waiting one bad moment away. He stripped his gloves. Stupid. Necessary.\n\n“Draw, don’t scoop,” Tiffani said, eyes on his hands. “Thin it. Keep it singing.”\n\nHe set his palm to the skin. It stuck instantly, devil-slick. Heat spoke bone. His fingers tried to curl. He refused them. He pulled not up but across, finding appetite the way he found it in pins. The glass came, skin into rope, rope into ribbon. Beneath it, the body remembered fluid. He gave it a path that wasn’t Tiffani.\n\nThe pain rewrote everything tender into function. Grip. Pull. Release. Again. When the glass complained in a high wire, he eased angle, gentled it. Tiffani shadowed his moves with words barely above breath—“yes… now… thinner”—as if speaking could file the edge.\n\nAt the gate, metal sulked then shifted. Chloen’s bar bit deeper; Bram bullied hinge and pin until metal remembered duty. The frame sprang a finger, then two.\n\n“Open,” Chloen said, and the pin surrendered all at once.\n\nTiffani slipped through, quick as a number. The catwalk gave another warning cry behind her—brace reconsidering—and Bram caught her collar for one hand-span while she found a rung. They hit the furnace floor together. Jhace pulled the last of the spill into harmless thread; some of his skin went with it and cooled there, a gift the glass would keep.\n\n“Wrap,” Bram said, already tearing his shirt into bands. Jhace let the cloth take the weeping heat. He didn’t look at his hands. Looking made pain louder.\n\nTiffani looked for him. “Thank you,” she said. Not big. Exact.\n\nA mullion cracked, whisper-clean. North panes, bowed all watch, decided to become knives. One sheet fell. Shiver, smash, a glittering weather. The room’s geometry lost patience.\n\n“Out,” Bram barked.\n\nThey moved—through the hot maintenance door, into a stair that smelled like brick and old soap. The floor practiced a tilt. At the landing, the west wall shifted a hand; part of it came with.\n\nChloen took the hit. Not crushed—caught. A beam knocked her to one knee. A long blade of glass, honest as math, drove into her flank and stopped, thinking. Air had trouble getting into her. Blood had no such trouble getting out.\n\nBram was there first, hands under her shoulders, cursing like prayer. Tiffani slid to the wound’s far side, fingers close but never touching. “Don’t pull,” she said. “We bind. Then we move.”\n\nJhace knelt. The third note pulsed harder here, a bass wire under the room. He put pressure high and wide; Tiffani bound; Chloen’s breath came back in small, ragged rental.\n\n“We’ve got you,” Bram said, voice filed flat. “We’ve got you.”\n\n“Quench-hall,” Tiffani said. “Two streets. We carry.”\n\nJhace finally looked at his hands. They were meat wrapped in wet cloth, fingers blistered into wrong shapes. They were also still his. He nodded, got under Chloen’s weight opposite Bram, and braced while Tiffani tied off the last band.\n\n“Move,” he said.\n\nThey went out into white dust and the bell grammar of a city choosing which part of itself to hold next.\n\nCHAPTER 5: COPPER-GOLD\n\nThe quench-hall had been cleared to stone and turned into a triage by moving basins aside and deciding the floor was now a ledger. Bodies lay where the tilt wouldn’t roll them. The air smelled like hot water, old blood, and stubbornness. Flags outside pointed crews toward worse places.\n\nThey set Chloen on a bench meant for cooling pontils. Her face held color again, but her breath went in as if charged rent. A quench-sister—glass thread braided into dark hair—arrived with the clipped calm of someone rationing mercy.\n\n“Stable?” she asked, already checking jaw and dressing. Her mouth tightened. “We’re full.”\n\n“She holds for half a watch,” Tiffani said, even. “We bought it.”\n\nThe sister’s gaze took in Jhace’s wrapped hands, the cloth already sticking, then fell—just once—on a small, strangely clean line visible along the inside of his right wrist where the wrap didn’t quite cover. She filed that away, said nothing.\n\n“Half a watch,” she agreed, and moved on to a man whose leg had reverted to idea.\n\nBram hovered with the helpless strength of a beam that couldn’t be put where it was needed. Jhace stood because sitting would mean finished. His hands hissed faintly where the wraps met wet air.\n\n“Cool the cloth,” Tiffani said, already filling a shallow dish. She held it under his fingers without touching. Steam lifted in mean little puffs. It helped enough to count.\n\n“You didn’t run,” he said, because credit is a brace.\n\n“Neither did you,” she said. They both pretended it was a joke.\n\nThe hall made bad music: coughs, choked cries, orders simplified to verbs. Bells shifted grammar outside: brace found, brace failed, crews turned. Under it, quieter but present now that he’d learned its shape, the third note threaded through ribs and stone.\n\nChloen’s eyes fluttered open. Brown, not glass-bright. She shaped a whisper. “First rule. We don’t die in training halls.”\n\n“This isn’t a hall,” Bram said, rough.\n\n“It is today,” she murmured, and closed her eyes to save the resource of wakefulness.\n\nThe sister came back with cleaner band and two braces carved from chair legs. “We’ll seat the glass edge tighter,” she said. “Buys you more watch.” Her hands moved with workman clarity. “Don’t do anything brave without me. Brave leaks.”\n\nThey lifted. Chloen’s breath snagged; her skin went the color of clean paper. The room’s edges pulled away a fraction, like distance had increased. All at once, the bind wasn’t enough. The blood at the dressing’s edge pushed with a new intent; the shallow breaths turned to coins hard to pay.\n\n“Hold,” Tiffani said, voice narrowed to a blade. Bram’s hands went still under Chloen’s shoulders.\n\nJhace’s body found a place it knew without naming. The old work-hum that lived under his ribs rose as if someone had tapped the forge bar and called it true. Not thought. Not a trick. A shape.\n\nHe set the wrapped meat of his palm an inch from the wound, not touching. He built the idea of copper-gold in his chest—a clean, ordered warmth that pulled things toward themselves instead of apart—and pressed that idea down his arm to the air between cloth and skin.\n\nSomething answered. Not the third note. An answer closer. The blood’s bright edge fuzzed, then steadied. Chloen’s next breath didn’t pay such a price.\n\nTiffani’s gaze flicked once to his wrist, then to his face. She said nothing, which was respect.\n\nJhace held the shape like a man holding a pin that wants to skitter. He smoothed the wound’s edge with intent, shaved a hair of wrongness until the two sides wanted to meet. Not sealing a lie—just refusing the gap. Breath by breath, the panic left Chloen’s ribs.\n\nCost arrived, precise. A tiny line etched along the inside of his right wrist under the wrap—cold, not heat—no longer than a seed and no wider than a hair. A copper-brown filament wrote itself in his skin with meticulous spite. He did not pull back. He let the mark exist.\n\nChloen’s eyes opened again. Focus returned like tide. “What was that?”\n\n“Bought you time,” he said.\n\n“Don’t spend it all in one place,” Bram muttered, and his hand trembled once on her shoulder, then stopped.\n\nThe sister saw enough to know what to say and what not to. “Whatever you’re doing,” she said to Jhace without looking, “don’t do it again in my hall. I need the room to keep breathing.”\n\n“Copy,” he said. The copper-gold shape shrank of its own accord; he let it go. If he pushed again, it would take more than a line.\n\nThey bound tighter over what he’d calmed. Color returned to Chloen by sips. Tiffani rinsed her hands in a basin that had given up on staying clean and dried them on a rag that used to be a festival banner. Her voice stayed practical. “Your hands.”\n\n“They’re mine,” he said. “They’ll look different.”\n\n“They’ll serve,” she said, deciding for both of them. She looked at the wraps like they were a map to study later. “What you did—” She cupped air, a vessel for nothing. “—wasn’t forcing. It was guiding to true. Wholeness.”\n\nThe word fit the coin-cool place under his ribs. He nodded, not trusting his mouth.\n\nA boy with a bell-stare stumbled in; the flags outside snapped to a new tense. The third note slackened a fraction, like the city had shifted its weight onto a different foot.\n\n“Eat when someone puts food in your hand,” Bram said, defaulting to orders he could control. “Drink when they yell. Sleep when a bell lies and says you can.”\n\n“Yes, Bram,” Tiffani said, and because it wasn’t mockery, something in him unknotted.\n\nChloen breathed, not rented now, owned. She grimaced. “Soon,” she said, proving she’d overheard too much.\n\n“Soon,” Bram answered.\n\nJhace looked down. Beneath the edge of soaked cloth, that new line on his wrist sat horizon-straight, copper-brown and no longer than a fingernail’s white. A first mark. A cost that would ask for companions.\n\nFlags outside sent crews east. The hall’s noise shifted from panic toward rhythm: a terrible song sung correctly. Across the room, someone tried to be brave and was set right. Nearer, a woman laughed once like metal ringing true and then went back to work.\n\nJhace met Tiffani’s eyes; they shared an entry in a ledger that had not existed this morning. Hands for a life. A life for a mark. He looked at Bram and at Chloen and at the list forming in his head: X-in-circle where they’d held; red hashes where the city needed fixing; one small copper dash at his wrist where something new had been asked and he’d said yes.\n\n“Work,” he said, blessing and curse both.\n\nWhen the bell lied and told them to rest, they pretended to believe it for a handful of breaths. Then they went back into a city still learning how much it weighed, and set their shoulders under what they could.\n\nINTERLUDE I: PRE-GOD STIRRINGS\n\nPART A: CORLEXI — PERFECT LINES\n\nPrism city was all angles pretending to be virtue. Corlexi loved it for that lie.\n\nHe stood on a ribbed gallery high in the half-built Spire of Measures, wind combed thin through the lattice, apprentices scattered below like commas in a sentence that would not end. Chalk lines webbed the floor. Plumb-threads hung perfectly still—not because the air was calm, but because he had refused motion.\n\n“Again,” he said.\n\nThey lifted the truss and set it into the mouth of the waiting frame. Blue-silver threaded his fingers and stayed, a constant wire singing under the skin. He pressed it into the join, not hard, not soft, exactly—until the two faces of steel forgot their seam. The hum in the Spire shifted half a note closer to satisfied.\n\n“Master,” Adren ventured, voice careful. “We’ve been at it three watches.”\n\n“We are not tired,” Corlexi said, and made the statement true in the way a line becomes true when enough force is applied. The boy straightened. Everyone did. Corlexi ignored the tremor that ran through Adren’s hands after, the small rebellion of meat. Hands lacked principles. He worked around them.\n\nThe city beyond lay sharpened by late glow, every roofline cutting the sky. He loved the certainty of edges. Loved the moment when a bracket’s petty war with gravity ended and the bracket conceded he had always been right. Disaster had a geometry; it could be designed against. If he perfected the forms, the world would remember how to hold itself.\n\nHe set a palm to the next joint. Blue-silver brightened. The glow had stopped being event and become background, like breath. A pleasant pressure gathered in his knuckles; his fingers did not want to fold. He didn’t require them to. He shaped the force through flat hands and let his wrists carry the line. Calcification is what the soft-minded called it. He preferred names that didn’t whine. Strength accruing where weakness had wasted time.\n\nA brace complained far below—soft, then quiet, chastened by a glance he didn’t bother to send. The apprentices had learned to feel his gaze without wasting his time receiving it.\n\n“Master,” said Meve, the only one with a spine he hadn’t had to fabricate. “The north run is a hair over-stiff. If wind takes a mood, we’ll get chatter.”\n\nHe liked her for that. “Then remove the hair,” he said. “After we seat this.”\n\nBlue-silver settled into the truss like a clean number. The Spire answered with a long, low approval. Corlexi let himself breathe. He imagined a city where every rib and brace and gutter held to this logic. Where no bulge in the sky could find purchase because there were no irregularities to amplify. Where people lived inside certainty and forgot the taste of fear.\n\nHis thumb would not touch his forefinger. He watched the failure to meet with mild curiosity, as if it belonged to someone else left on his scaffold by mistake. He tried the other hand. Same result. Stiffness. Clean, bright pain when he insisted. He smiled at it, fond. A ledger line, not a warning.\n\nAdren staggered—just a step—then caught himself on a girder and turned the stumble into a stretch with the gracelessness of a liar. Meve saw. Corlexi saw Meve see and permitted her the mercy of not making her confess fatigue.\n\n“Water,” he said, magnanimous. “A swallow. Then the west truss.”\n\nRelief moved across the crew like wind over grass. He let it. They drank. They breathed. The blue-silver under his skin did not dim. It had learned he had use for it always.\n\nWhen work resumed, Meve adjusted the north run by a finger’s wisdom and the whole lattice sang freer. Corlexi nodded once. She would be useful when the math grew harder.\n\nHe looked out over Prism, at the angles folding toward night, and told the city the truth he had decided: if form were perfect, catastrophe would have nowhere to root. He set his palm to steel again and made a promise to the air he would keep with bone if asked.\n\nPerfection requires sacrifice. He had always been willing to pay.\n\nPART B: MOBEL — CLEANSING FIRE\n\nThe desert at Heattide had a way of stripping the polite off things. Mobel liked the honesty.\n\nHis tent was a shadow pinned to hard ground, canvas singing quietly under a sun that refused to age. The woman on the pallet had a wound along the thigh that smelled like old rot turned argumentative. Her husband knelt beside her with hope stretched thin between both hands, the way men hold rope when the beam is already failing.\n\nMobel washed his fingers in water he could have drunk and did not. He wiped them dry and brought them over the wound. White-gold licked up from his palms with the inevitability of sunrise. The heat had a voice; it spoke of purity and endings. He let it speak through him.\n\nThe woman screamed once, clean and strong. He closed his eyes to save her the indignity of seeing them open while she learned what clean meant. “Hold her,” he said, and the husband obeyed, weeping apology into her hair as if that could dilute the necessity.\n\nFlesh bubbled, then flattened. The stink lifted and left something metallic and sharp behind. The white-gold narrowed to a blade-thin line and walked the wound’s edge, burning away anything that argued with health. “Contamination requires fire,” Mobel said, as he said for all, a simple litany to steady the hands. “Purity requires pain.”\n\nOutside the tent, a cluster of watchers held their breath in a single borrowed chest. They had followed him here—quiet women with hard faces; men with scars and eyes that had stopped searching for soft places; two youths too young to be certain about anything and hungry for someone who seemed to be. They liked the clarity he offered. He liked that they liked it. It made the work simpler.\n\nThe white-gold receded. The wound shone, raw and clean, edges crisp as a fresh-cut pane. He wrapped it in cloth soaked with boiled herbs and nothing that smelled like earth. The woman sobbed into the cool of the bandage and then, exhausted, slept.\n\nHer husband tried to kiss Mobel’s hands. Mobel lifted them out of reach. Something in the skin had swollen—a pressure under the knuckles like water seeking a seam. He flexed and felt the complaint spread along the tendons like a fault line mapping itself. He filed it where it belonged: a note. Costs were inventory; inventory was comfort.\n\nA boy at the tent mouth, dust to the eyebrows, said, “She’ll live?”\n\n“She will not rot,” Mobel said. “The rest is her labor, not mine.”\n\nThe boy nodded like he’d been given permission to hand his fear to the woman and make her carry it. Mobel didn’t correct him. The desert corrected everything eventually; he didn’t compete with it.\n\nA woman in the knot of followers pressed forward with a bundle wrapped in cloth. “For you,” she said, and unfolded a loaf that remembered being bread earlier in the watch, and a small jar of oil that gleamed like a promise.\n\n“I don’t eat after the work,” he said. “It blurs the edge.” He meant it. Food softened. He had no use for soft.\n\nThey backed away, chastened, then more devout. He saw it move through them—how denial becomes doctrine when hunger is watching—and set it aside. It wasn’t his task to manage their hearts. Only his own.\n\nHe stepped out into the sun because he liked the way it made the white-gold inside him feel less like fire and more like law. The desert’s wind picked up and the tent answered with a different song, one that had learned the third note that threaded the world since the bulge in the sky had come and passed. Mobel ignored it. His note was cleaner. He had chosen it.\n\nHe looked east, where the horizon’s line cut pure across the sand, and thought of the cities with their filth and their compromises. Thought of the way rot hid in corners and called itself neighborly. He rested his hands, swollen and honest, against the tent pole until the ache made a proper accounting.\n\n“Only the pure will survive what’s coming,” he said, not loudly. The watchers heard. Words moved through them like a heat-front, leaving belief behind in their wake.\n\nHe went back inside to boil more cloth, to pour more water he would not drink, to answer one more body with the fire it deserved."
          ]
        },

    "538d841f-c8a4-4e4b-b45b-2a61b04dd402": {
      "children": [
        "a9610f02-4172-48c7-ab58-756839ac5bfc"
      ],
      "id": "538d841f-c8a4-4e4b-b45b-2a61b04dd402",
      "message": {

        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "### CHAPTER 13: MORNING-OF\n\nMorning watch put a crowd on the rib whether anyone had invited it or not.\n\nHeat-Rib 3 was a black line against a pale sky; ropes already tied where men pretended they wouldn’t fall; bells hung head-high with chalk under them: eyes, hands, back. The captain in leathers stalked the catwalk with her usual tray-balanced authority. Bram marked a small circle of work with his body. Runners moved like bright nerves along the path they’d set yesterday. Two hundred people breathed in one slow, expectant lung.\n\nTiffani flexed her fingers and the joints did not answer. Oil had stopped being persuasion; it was theater. Blue-silver did not spark at her knuckles; stiffness held, cold and precise. She could lay marks. She could teach. She could not guarantee the small nudges that made a hanger give up its last rattle and call the seam true. Not at this watch. Not with hands that had decided to argue with her.\n\nJhace took the chalk without her asking. He laid X-in-circle on the rail for practice—clean enough—and the double-bar beside it. His right hand buzzed a mild buzz he refused to dignify. The copper-brown line inside his wrist sat a finger-joint long, neat as law. It had not lengthened in the night. For now that counted.\n\nThe hum along the rib was steady as a held breath. The third note, quieter, still tapped a patient finger on the bones of anyone honest enough to admit it.\n\n“Crowd’s bigger than we wanted,” Bram said.\n\n“We wanted none,” Tiffani said.\n\n“You got a city,” the captain said, overhearing, not apologizing. “And a wall that listens when you talk. Teach fast.”\n\nTiffani looked at the hinge they’d chosen for demonstration. Honest hinge. Willing to be taught in public. She looked at her hands and said the number they were pretending not to be: “Not enough.”\n\nJhace heard it without the word. He set the chalk down and came to stand where they could talk in plain air that would not be recorded by anyone’s ledger. Close, a half-hand off. “I can carry your line,” he said. “You set the angle. I hold. You don’t have to do the whole thing.”\n\n“Wholeness isn’t Form,” she said, precise even with fear. “Your warmth invites. My work refuses. They don’t stack. Not safely.”\n\n“Then I’ll reinforce under your refusal,” he said. “A coin of warmth under the edge you’re turning. Not more. You make true. I keep it from slipping while you make it.”\n\n“Cross-spend could pull you sideways,” she said. “You just learned to stop.”\n\n“Then we stop when you say.” He was calm in the way steel is calm—because it has already chosen not to move. “If it costs me, it costs me. I’m not letting you stand in front of two hundred people with hands that won’t do the thing they think they’re here to see you do.”\n\n“That’s not how precision works,” she said.\n\n“It is how partnership does,” he said.\n\nShe did the math. Hands: compromised. Crowd: volatile. Hinge: public. His vein-mark: unforgiving. The page on **Stopping** pinned to the wall in her head: three cues—her call, his feel, the line. She needed him under her work like a brace she could trust not to wander.\n\n“It might kill you,” she said, because honesty was a tool she used even when she hated its weight.\n\n“Then we die together,” he said. He didn’t make it soft. He made it exact.\n\nShe flinched—small, involuntary—at the sentence that fit and could not be allowed to become a habit. She set a palm flat to his chest, not for tenderness—measurement. His breath under her hand was steady enough to build on. She put her forehead to his and made them share a line.\n\n“Breathe,” she said.\n\nThey matched breaths until the count stopped feeling like control and started feeling like rhythm. Forehead to forehead, weight matched, two structures aligned by choice. His tremor smoothed to a whisper the bones could ignore. The ache in her thumb shifted from glass-cold to merely difficult. The world around them decided to wait without being told to.\n\n“Say it,” he said, quiet as chalk on slate.\n\nShe had never wasted language. She didn’t start now. “Whatever happens,” she said, voice clean and unslanted, “I love you.”\n\nHe didn’t reach for metaphor or banter. He didn’t make it grand. He set his mouth near her temple like you set a tool back in its place after use and said, “I know. Me too.”\n\nThey let their foreheads rest one more breath, then separated to where work lived.\n\nBram took the rope that made their circle a fact. “Coins,” he reminded, for both of them.\n\n“Coins,” Jhace said, and looked to her.\n\n“On my breath,” Tiffani said. “You stop when I say even if your bones argue.”\n\n“Copy.”\n\nThey stepped into the small space the captain had kept clear and let two hundred people become wall. Tiffani laid chalk marks on the hinge: arrow where alignment started, square for **align first**, small note under the bell marked *hands*. She lifted her ruined, excellent hand and made the angle in the air as much with her body as with her fingers.\n\n“Lift,” she said to the crew. “Not pry. On three.” She didn’t count. She breathed once and Jhace took that as one; he fed the smallest coin of warmth under the edge she was refusing into true—just enough to make the metal consider agreeing.\n\nHer thumb seized at the worst moment. He felt the slip begin and set a second coin under it without waiting for pride to comment. She hated the help and took it. “Now,” she said, and Bram quarter-turned while the hum was willing. The hinge gave a finger of truth.\n\n“Stop,” she said immediately.\n\nHe stopped. The coin cooled where he told it to. The third note pushed. He didn’t let it write.\n\nAnother breath. “Again,” she said.\n\nThey did it. Coins, not pours. Under her line, not over it. Twice more, with the crowd learning by watching that the work was not a trick but an obedience to small instructions.\n\nShe felt it when the hinge had taken enough. “Mark,” she said, and a wallman drew X-in-circle, then the double-bar, thick as vow, hands steadier for having had a job.\n\nThe captain grinned a thin wire. “Good,” she said. “Again down-line for the fools too far to see it the first time.”\n\n“Two spans,” Tiffani said, already measuring the next. “Not sermons.”\n\nJhace rolled his sleeve to check truth. The line on his wrist had not lengthened. He tugged the fabric down quick so the part of his brain that made bargains wouldn’t try to make one now.\n\nThey stood together—close enough to borrow balance without stealing it—and turned as one to face the rib proper. The hum under Heat-Rib 3 shifted half a breath, as if the city had leaned forward to listen. Flags cracked loose from themselves and remembered they were supposed to say things. A bell found the pattern that meant *eyes*.\n\n“Ready?” Bram asked, which was ridiculous and necessary.\n\n“Ready,” Tiffani said.\n\n“Ready,” Jhace answered.\n\nThey stepped toward the demonstration line and two hundred people followed the motion with their attention. The morning had chosen. The wall would learn. Or they would. They didn’t look back. They faced Heat-Rib 3."
          ]
        },

    "53b59827-c62f-48ad-90f7-7ec2f6cafc51": {
      "children": [
        "f043670e-659b-4859-9a99-5d888e430699"
      ],

    "54e5e8b3-4951-4c0b-9422-9639fc80fe96": {
      "children": [
        "957b9970-bd9b-4510-b7e6-58cf30110a0b"
      ],
      "id": "54e5e8b3-4951-4c0b-9422-9639fc80fe96",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# Pass 5 — Interlude I Knowledge-Gate Fix + Horror Upgrade\r\n# 1) Replace Interlude I with a compliant, more ominous version.\r\n# 2) Run a quick forbidden-term scan (\"white-gold\", \"Purity\", \"Entropy\") to ensure N01 gate compliance.\r\n\r\nfrom pathlib import Path\r\nimport re\r\nfrom docx import Document\r\n\r\nsrc = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds-PASS2-3-4.md\")\r\ntext = src.read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\n\r\n# New, compliant Interlude I content (no Purity/Entropy naming; Corlexi uses Structure only; Mobel uses mundane cautery & doctrine)\r\nnew_interlude = \"\"\"INTERLUDE I: PRE-GOD STIRRINGS\r\n\r\nPART A: CORLEXI — PERFECT LINES\r\n\r\nPrism was all angles pretending to be virtue. Corlexi loved it for that lie.\r\n\r\nHe stood on a ribbed gallery high in the half-built Spire of Measures, wind combed thin through the lattice, apprentices scattered below like commas in a sentence that would not end. Chalk lines webbed the floor. Plumb-threads hung perfectly still—not because the air was calm, but because he refused motion.\r\n\r\n“Again,” he said.\r\n\r\nThey lifted the truss and set it into the mouth of the waiting frame. Blue-silver threaded his fingers and stayed, a constant wire under the skin. He pressed it into the join—not hard, not soft, exactly—until the two faces of steel forgot their seam. The Spire’s hum shifted half a note closer to satisfied.\r\n\r\n“Master,” Adren ventured, careful. “We’ve been at it three watches.”\r\n\r\n“We are not tired,” Corlexi said, and made the statement feel true the way a line becomes true when enough force is applied. The boy straightened. Everyone did.\r\n\r\nA brace complained far below—soft, then quiet, chastened by a glance Corlexi did not bother to send. The city beyond lay sharpened by late glow, every roofline cutting the sky. He loved the certainty of edges. Loved the moment when a bracket’s petty war with gravity ended and the bracket conceded he had always been right. Disaster had a geometry; it could be designed against. If he perfected the forms, the world would remember how to hold itself.\r\n\r\nThey shifted the west run. Adren’s boot slipped on chalk and oil. He snatched at air, found nothing, tilted. Meve lunged, caught a sleeve, lost purchase. Corlexi moved a hand-width—no farther—caught Adren’s collar, kept the line of the truss with his other hand as if both tasks were one equation. Blue-silver whispered at his knuckles where they touched steel. The apprentice hung a breath over the drop, feet bicycling emptily.\r\n\r\n“Still,” Corlexi said, as if calming a beam. Adren froze. Corlexi set the boy upright without permitting the truss to skew a single finger off true. The metal sang compliance. He released the collar last.\r\n\r\n“Thank you, Master,” Adren said, scarce air and shame in it.\r\n\r\n“Do not thank me for maintaining form,” Corlexi said. “Thank the form for permitting you to remain inside it.”\r\n\r\nThey worked on. The glow that had begun as event sat now like background, like breath. A pleasant pressure gathered in Corlexi’s knuckles; his fingers did not want to fold. He did not require them to. He shaped force through flat hands and let his wrists carry the line. Hands that lacked discipline shook; structures did not. He preferred structures.\r\n\r\nMeve squinted along the north run. “A hair over-stiff there. If wind takes a mood, we’ll get chatter.”\r\n\r\n“Remove the hair,” Corlexi said, “after we seat this.”\r\n\r\nBlue-silver settled into the truss like a clean number. The Spire answered with a long, low approval. Corlexi let himself breathe.\r\n\r\nHe imagined a city where every rib and brace and gutter held to this logic. Where bulges in the sky—whatever they were—could find no purchase because there were no irregularities to amplify. Where people lived inside certainty and forgot the taste of fear.\r\n\r\nHis thumb would not touch his forefinger. He watched the failure to meet with mild curiosity, as if it belonged to some other man left on his scaffold by mistake. He tried the other hand. Same result. Stiffness; bright, clean pain when he insisted. A ledger line, not a warning.\r\n\r\n“Water,” he said at last, magnanimous. “A swallow. Then the west truss.”\r\n\r\nRelief moved across the crew like wind over grass. He let it. They drank. They breathed. The blue-silver under his skin did not dim.\r\n\r\nPerfection requires sacrifice. He had always been willing to pay.\r\n\r\nPART B: MOBEL — CLEANSING FIRE\r\n\r\nThe desert at Heattide stripped the polite off things. Mobel liked the honesty.\r\n\r\nHis tent was a shadow pinned to hard ground, canvas singing under a sun that refused to age. The woman on the pallet had a wound along the thigh that smelled like rot turned argumentative. Her husband knelt beside her with hope stretched thin between both hands, the way men hold rope when the beam is already failing.\r\n\r\nMobel washed his fingers in water he could have drunk and did not. He wiped them dry and worked with what he had: boiled cloth, clean band, a narrow iron warmed in coals until it went the color of admission. Heat has a voice; it speaks of endings. He let it speak through his hand.\r\n\r\n“Hold her,” he said.\r\n\r\nThe woman screamed once, clean and strong. Flesh bubbled, then flattened. The stink lifted and left something metallic and sharp behind. He walked the wound’s edge with care, burning away anything that argued with health. “Contamination requires fire,” Mobel said, as he said for all, a simple litany to steady hands. “Clean is the law.”\r\n\r\nOutside the tent, a knot of watchers held their breath in a single borrowed chest. They had followed him here—quiet women with hard faces; men with scars and eyes that had stopped searching for soft places; two youths too young to be certain and hungry for someone who seemed to be. They liked the clarity he offered. He liked that they liked it. It made the work simpler.\r\n\r\nHe bound the wound in boiled cloth. The woman sobbed into the cool and then, exhausted, slept. Her husband reached to kiss Mobel’s hands. Mobel lifted them out of reach. The skin had swollen from the iron—a pressure under the knuckles like water seeking a seam. He flexed and felt the complaint spread along the tendons like a fault line mapping itself. He filed it as inventory. Costs were comfort when counted correctly.\r\n\r\nA second couple pushed forward. The man’s forearm was banded with ink that crawled into an old cut. The skin around it went the wrong colors. “Please,” the woman said, throat hoarse. “We walked a reach.”\r\n\r\nMobel looked at the arm, then at the ink. “You kept filth in the skin,” he said, not loud. “You chose it.”\r\n\r\n“It’s my clan,” the man rasped. “Not filth.”\r\n\r\n“Then let your clan heal you,” Mobel said, and stepped aside. The watchers murmured—approval more than anger, relief at a rule applied. The woman begged again, soft, the way people do when their words have no leverage left.\r\n\r\nMobel did not compete with the desert. The desert corrected everything eventually.\r\n\r\nHe stepped into the sun because he liked the way heat made the ache in his hands feel less like pain and more like law. Wind picked up; the tent answered with a different song, one that had learned the low third note threading the world since the sky had bulged and passed. Mobel ignored it. His note was simpler: clean or not.\r\n\r\nHe looked east, where the horizon cut pure across sand, and thought of cities with their compromises. Thought of the way rot hid in corners and called itself neighborly. He rested swollen hands against the tent pole until the ache made a proper accounting.\r\n\r\n“Only the clean will stand,” he said. Words moved through the watchers like a heat-front, leaving belief behind in their wake.\r\n\r\nThen he went back in to boil more cloth, to pour more water he would not drink, to answer one more body with the fire it deserved.\r\n\"\"\"\r\n\r\n# Replace the Interlude block\r\npattern = r\"INTERLUDE I:.*?(?=^Chapter\\s+\\d+:|\\Z)\"\r\nnew_text, n = re.subn(pattern, new_interlude, text, flags=re.DOTALL|re.MULTILINE)\r\nassert n >= 1, \"Interlude I section not found for replacement.\"\r\ntext = new_text\r\n\r\n# Quick forbidden-term scan\r\nforbidden = [\"white-gold\", \"Purity\", \"Entropy\"]\r\nhits = {term: [] for term in forbidden}\r\nfor term in forbidden:\r\n    for m in re.finditer(term, text, flags=re.IGNORECASE):\r\n        # capture a short snippet around the match\r\n        s = max(0, m.start()-40); e = min(len(text), m.end()+40)\r\n        hits[term].append(text[s:e])\r\n\r\n# Save updated manuscript\r\nout_md = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds-PASS5-InterludeI-Fixed.md\")\r\nout_md.write_text(text, encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\n\r\ndoc = Document()\r\nfor line in text.split(\"\\n\"):\r\n    doc.add_paragraph(line)\r\nout_docx = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds-PASS5-InterludeI-Fixed.docx\")\r\ndoc.save(str(out_docx))\r\n\r\n# Write scan report\r\nreport_lines = [\"# Pass 5 — Interlude I Fix + Forbidden-term Scan\", \"## Scan Results\"]\r\nfor term, snippets in hits.items():\r\n    report_lines.append(f\"\\n### '{term}' occurrences: {len(snippets)}\")\r\n    for snip in snippets[:5]:\r\n        report_lines.append(f\"- …{snip}…\")\r\nscan_path = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Pass5-ScanReport.md\")\r\nscan_path.write_text(\"\\n\".join(report_lines), encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\n\r\nprint(\"Updated manuscript:\", out_md)\r\nprint(\"DOCX:\", out_docx)\r\nprint(\"Scan report:\", scan_path)"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1763207534.963078,
        "end_turn": true,
