The hinge gave a finger of truth back.\n\n“Small,” she said toward Jhace without looking. “Coins. Only coins.”\n\nHis warmth fed the upstream join like a held coin laid on a ledger. The buckle downstream paused, then unsaid itself by halves. She felt the spine’s note drop a hand, then another, until it matched the rib around it and stopped pretending to be a god.\n\nThe corner held. The crowd’s breath came back like weather correcting.\n\nShe went to her knees before she chose to and found him already sliding to a sit, back to the rail, fingers buzzing in angry saw-tooth. He turned his right wrist and showed her what the work had taken.\n\nThe copper-brown filament that had lived hair-thin along his inner wrist had leapt. Not a seed-width. Not a fingernail’s moon. A finger-joint long now, dark and exact, as if someone had drawn it into him with a compass and spite. Cool burn. New grammar written under skin.\n\n“Count,” she said, because numbers are rails you can trust. “With me.”\n\n“One,” he said, hoarse.\n\n“Your name.”\n\n“Jhace.”\n\n“My name.”\n\nHis mouth erased for a breath, then found it. “Tiffani.”\n\nShe let her hand hover near his cheek without touching, the way you hold heat over glass to read it. “Again.”\n\nHe said both names until the third note retreated to the cheap seats. The cold left his palms last. Bram crouched in the circle they’d made, a hinge of a man making a safer geometry around a smaller one.\n\n“You overreached,” Bram said, gentler than a rebuke. “Tried to be two crews.”\n\n“I saw it,” Tiffani said, cutting off the apology forming in Jhace’s throat because apology was wasted energy. “We will measure earlier next time. Or I will put you on your back sooner.”\n\nThe captain in leathers looked down at them with a face that didn’t know softness and had learned respect. “You held,” she said to the work, not the man. Then, to him: “Don’t do that again. I need you alive.”\n\nTiffani stood on legs that pretended they had always been steady. She handed the chalk to a wallman whose hand shook and watched him draw X-in-circle, double-bar. She added a small hash on the hinge seat—her private notation for a place that had tried to kill people once and should not be trusted alone. The ache in her thumb had gone glass-cold; she flexed, rubbed once, and set the hand again. Cost: logged.\n\nThey made two streets before Jhace’s knees remembered they could be optional; Bram took the heavier kit with the casual dominance of a man built to be furniture until balance returns. Tiffani matched pace, listening to the frame of him—breath rate, footfall, the subtle drift of someone recalibrating.\n\nAt the forge, the floor-map was scuffed into a palimpsest. Chloen saw the line on Jhace’s wrist and did not ask the wrong questions. She fetched water and cloth and sat on the floor with the kind of grace you earn by arguing your body into compliance.\n\n“Say it again,” Tiffani told him, oiling her thumb with a sting that made her blink once and act as if she hadn’t. “All of it.”\n\n“You don’t have to hold everything,” he said, the sentence fitting better on second casting.\n\n“That’s what I’m here for,” she finished, softer, and the room took it like a mark it could read—new grammar, added to the manual.\n\nHe closed his eyes. She watched the tremor settle from saw-tooth to mosquito-whine. The line along his wrist stayed a finger-joint long, copper-dark, precise. Fear named itself in her chest and sat obediently where she put it.\n\n“We’ll write this,” she said, to Bram, to Chloen, mostly to herself. “A page for spending and stopping. A page for when to tackle an idiot.”\n\nBram snorted, relief carefully disguised. “Make the pictures loud.”\n\n“We will.” She looked back to Jhace. “Look at me.”\n\nHe did. Clear this time. Present.\n\n“Copy?” she asked.\n\n“Copy,” he said.\n\nOutside, the city shifted its weight and decided, for the moment, not to fall. The third note threaded under everything, humbled but not gone. Tiffani laid the chalk on the table and sat within reach of tools. When the bell lied about rest, she would pretend to believe it long enough to make belief true. Then she would get up and teach him, again, where to stop—and she would be there when he forgot.\n\n---\n\n### INTERLUDE II: RUMOR & REPORT\n\n#### PART A: SALEE — EVERYONE’S PAIN\n\nCauldron-Hold called itself a clinic again because someone had wiped the ash off the sign. Salee didn’t argue with the name. Names kept people walking in.\n\nThey came in bent and left a little taller. That was the work. She took the worst of it.\n\nA line trailed out past the basins to the alley mouth—old burns that had learned to ache, fresh breaks that hadn’t learned manners, fevers that talked in riddles. She moved from pallet to pallet with a wet cloth and clean thread and the quiet voice that made people remember how to breathe. When her hand rested near a wound, the room tilted. Heat shifted. Pain leaned toward her like metal toward a magnet and then slid, obedient, under her skin.\n\nThe first man had a rib that wouldn’t let him breathe all the way. She set her palm near the bruise and listened. The tightness gathered like a fist and then unfurled itself into her. He exhaled to the bottom for the first time in a tide. “Bless you,” he said, astonished. She nodded and went to the next.\n\nBy watch’s end she had a tally of blessings, a basin of thread ends, and two forearms that had learned a new map: copper-brown lines writing themselves with neat malice, curving from palm-heels toward elbow’s bend. She washed them with the same cloth she used on other people and didn’t look long. Looking made the ache louder.\n\nA woman came in carrying a child who had not decided whether to stay. Salee took the weight, took the small hand, and let the room tilt again. The child’s eyes widened, then softened. The mother went to her knees and held Salee’s skirt like it was rope.\n\n“You’re taking it,” the mother whispered.\n\n“I’m borrowing it,” Salee said, which was the same lie she told herself. Pain didn’t return on its own. You had to hand it back, and she never remembered how.\n\nBetween patients, she checked the ledger. Not numbers; a list of names and short notes in a cramped hand: **Marn—breath**, **Fex—hand**, **Old Juri—sleep**. The list steadied her. It made the ache orderly.\n\nA boy with glass-shy in his palm flinched from the basin. “It will hurt,” he said, already ashamed.\n\n“It will,” she agreed. “Then it won’t.” She took his wrist, set her other hand near the glittering specks, and invited the sting to change direction. It came obediently, quick and bright. For a breath she couldn’t feel her own palm at all. When sensation returned, it was thinner, like light through milk.\n\n“Better?” she asked.\n\nHe nodded, surprised at his own courage, and left the basin grinning. She added **Ktt—shy** to the ledger.\n\nBy late watch, her hands trembled in a fine, irritating buzz. Someone offered her a cup. She smiled and forgot to drink it. People thanked her with bread, with buttons, with the way they stood up straighter. She stacked thanks on the shelf with the thread. The shelf sagged.\n\nAt the door, a man with a soldier’s old back asked, not unkindly, “What do you keep for yourself?”\n\nSalee looked at her palms—the lines a little darker now, climbing with diligent intent—and tried to recall the last time her body had complained in her own voice. The memory ducked out of reach like a shy animal.\n\n“I don’t remember what my pain feels like,” she said, and surprised herself with the truth of it.\n\nThe man bowed as if that were a sacred answer and left before she could take the weight of his gratitude.\n\nWhen the bell lied about rest, she turned the sign anyway. The alley grumbled; the city did not lack for injuries. A girl with fever stood already in the doorway, swaying.\n\nSalee raised her hands. The lines glowed dull and did not ask permission.\n\n“If I take all the pain,” she told the room, not looking to see who heard, “no one has to suffer.”\n\nNo one argued. She went back to work.\n\n---\n\n#### PART B: ERIES — ENDINGS ARE NATURAL\n\nVenn Eries chose a quiet table beneath the temple’s unlit lamp and arranged his pages with care. Ink, straightedge, a neat stack of fools’ comfort. He called it a treatise to make the shaking in his fingers feel like industry.\n\n*On the Necessity of Endings.*\n\nHe wrote the title again because the first time was untidy. Then a sentence that had been waiting behind his teeth since the Piercing: *Continuance without purpose is cruelty.* He paused, not for thought, but to measure the breath it took to commit the words to the page. Breath was a unit you could count; grief was not.\n\nNovices swept ash into orderly lines and avoided his table. The elders did not. They came in a cluster—polite, troubled, already decided.\n\n“Venn,” said the oldest, whose hands had buried more people than most storms. “You are scaring the mourners.”\n\n“I am consoling them,” Eries said, and put a period down with exact force. “I am teaching that endings are not failures. They are the mercy of design.”\n\n“A philosophy of surrender,” another said.\n\n“A philosophy of kindness,” Eries replied. He looked up and let them see how tired his eyes had grown in so few tides. “Release is a form of love. To force a grip past its honesty is violence.”\n\nThey flinched at *violence*—a temple word, not to be used on priests. The oldest set a gentle palm on the page without smearing the ink. “You are dismissed from the hall,” he said, voice good at mercy. “Take your pages with you. Do not bring them back.”\n\nEjection did not feel like a fall. It felt like someone taking a weight off a beam already bowed. Eries gathered the treatise, left the lamp unlit, and walked into a street that had learned to speak in bells.\n\nBy the temple steps, three people waited as if they had known exactly when he would be thrown out. A woman with a healer’s hands and the tremor of someone who hadn’t slept. A man with a laborer’s shoulders wearing grief like a wet coat. A boy with ash on his eyelashes and no one’s hand to hold.\n\n“Are you Venn Eries?” the woman asked. The boy stared at the pages.\n\n“I am,” he said, surprised to hear his name still fit.\n\n“We heard you tell a woman to let go,” the man said, wary and hopeful in the same breath. “That she didn’t have to keep her father breathing through the night if there wasn’t any of him left to come back.”\n\n“She hit me,” Eries said, and found a smile that didn’t belong to cruelty. “Then she slept.”\n\nThe boy nodded like he understood something adults often hid from him. “Can you teach us,” he asked, “how to do… that? The letting?”\n\n“I can name it,” Eries said. “I can make it less frightening.”\n\nHe led them to a table that was not a temple’s. In the corner of a bread-shop that had decided to be a school for a watch, he opened the treatise and read the first paragraph aloud. People turned their heads; a few sat down without asking why.\n\nAs he spoke, his skin felt a size too large, like a garment you keep wearing for memory’s sake. The vitality leak was not dramatic—no gasp, no glamour—only a steady thinness, like pouring a jar in slow drops that never seemed to end. The woman’s eyes tracked it with the disgust of a practitioner who knew costs and hated to be grateful for them.\n\n“This will age you,” she said after the reading, blunt as duty.\n\n“It already has,” Eries said, and lifted his hand to show her the truth: veins a little darker, spots of chill under the skin where warmth didn’t bother staying. “Endings always do.”\n\nElders came to the shop door and hesitated—authority didn’t like rooms that named its limits. Passersby paused, listened, and found themselves sitting. A man who’d carried his mother’s body in his arms took the chair closest to the treatise and cried without apology. Eries handed him a cloth and kept reading.\n\n*Release is the kindest gift,* he wrote on a fresh page, to make sure the sentence would exist even if the bread-shop burned and the temple forgot him.\n\nSomeone asked from the back, scared and angry at once: “If everything ends, why build? Why love?”\n\n“Because endings make meaning,” Eries said, voice steady enough to carry. “Not less. More.”\n\nHe did not look back at the temple. He didn’t need its lamp. Outside, the city tested its braces and held, for now, at the edge of a proof it was not ready to see. Eries stacked his pages neatly, felt the years add their quiet weight, and accepted the arithmetic.\n\n“All things end,” he told the room, and the room believed him because he looked the part. “Why fight it?”\n\n---\n\n### CHAPTER 12: DRIFT ALIGN\n\nThe forge had been turned back into a room that could stand to be quiet. The floor-map was a ghost under fresh sweep-chalk; the anvil held a stack of stamped slips pretending to be authority; the rafters kept the day’s heat like a promise it might break later.\n\nJhace sat at the table with a stub of charcoal and the manual’s first page. His right hand shook in a fine, irritating buzz. The copper-brown line inside his wrist—new length, finger-joint exact—felt cool the way water feels cold when your teeth already hurt. He set his forearm on the wood to steal steadiness from it. The letters behaved.\n\n“Marks,” Tiffani said, pulling a chair with the care a person reserves for something she plans to use again. She had ink and a straightedge, a bottle with a mean stopper, and a small jar of oil that made her knuckles loosen a hair when she looked at it. “X-in-circle first. Then double-bar. Then arrows.” She met his eyes to make sure he’d heard. “Then the page on stopping.”\n\nHe huffed something like a laugh. “Thought I’d lead with ‘spend until collapse.’”\n\n“You tried that,” she said, and her mouth did a precise, unfriendly thing that still counted as affection. “It lacks elegance.”\n\nHe drew. X with the circle sung tight around it; his tremor made the first line a hair off. He lifted, reset, drew through the shake. The symbol read true enough. He wrote, in a blocky hand that had learned dignity from rivet tags: **done, checked**. Beneath it: **double-bar = checked under load**. He wanted a better word than load because the city was under something larger, but load was the word that bought obedience.\n\nTiffani copied clean beside him, her ink lines firm as spine. Blue-silver did not light at her knuckles; she didn’t spend for penwork. She did flex her left thumb twice before the bottle’s cork surrendered, and rubbed a sting of oil into the base of the joint like a person filing a burr off a tool. Cost: logged.\n\n“Page two,” she said. “Arrows. Where warmth invites. Where alignment starts.”\n\n“Small arrows,” he said, and drew them smaller than pride would want, so crews wouldn’t see a target big enough to lie to.\n\n“Page three,” she said, eyes on him rather than the paper. “Stopping.”\n\nHe looked at his wrist because honesty counted even when no one else was in the room. “Stopping,” he said, and the word tasted like a bolt you’d over-tightened and were trying to back off without snapping.\n\nShe reached across and set the flat of her hand next to his, not touching, parallel as rails. “We need three cues,” she said. “My call. Your feel. The mark.”\n\nHe nodded, listening to the idea like you listen to a plate. “Your call: **Stop. Coins.**”\n\n“My call,” she confirmed. “Your feel?”\n\n“The buzz goes saw-tooth,” he said. “Vision shifts at the edge like heat haze. The third note gets… persuasive.”\n\nShe wrote it without flinching at how ugly it looked in ink. “And the mark?”\n\nHe turned his wrist. The line sat there like a rule. “When it moves, we stop. Not negotiate. Stop.”\n\n“Good,” she said, and made it a line item, ordinary to the point of reverence. “We teach crews to point at your wrist before they ask you for anything.”\n\n“Bram already does,” he said.\n\n“Bram uses smaller words,” she said, and let the corner of her mouth relax the smallest breath.\n\nHe drew the last arrow and set the stub down. The buzz in his fingers kept going the way kettles keep talking after you lift them off heat. He anchored his hand with the other and watched the tremor notch down slowly, like a clutch finding.\n\n“You saved me,” he said.\n\n“Copy,” she said. No ceremony.\n\n“I didn’t know your name,” he said, and found there was space in his chest to say it in clean air. “For a breath. I looked at you and knew… the angle, the weight, the way your shoulder sits when you’re about to lie to a plate. But the word was gone.”\n\n“That’s what it does,” she said, precise even with fear. “I don’t care what we call it. We don’t let it do it again.”\n\nHe nodded. A bell far down-crown muttered in a grammar he didn’t need to answer yet. The forge door was cracked to let not-wind in; sounds slipped under it like shy cats—carriers, a distant argument turning into instructions, the captain’s voice somewhere making work behave.\n\nOn the table, the pages began to look like a language. Tiffani’s neat blocks; his blocky script; diagrams that would make sense to hands too tired for paragraphs. He wrote: **Coins, not pours**. Then underlined it, not twice, but with a line firm enough to be heard by men who ignored softer lines.\n\n“Show me,” she said after a time.\n\nHe looked up. “What.”\n\n“How you stop when it wants you,” she said, and lifted her chin toward the small bracket they’d left on the table for tests. “At three breaths. I’ll call.”\n\nHe hated and loved her for that. He set his palms near the seam. Copper-gold rose, obedient, small as a coin in a poor man’s pocket. The bracket answered, easing toward itself.\n\n“One,” she said.\n\nHe kept it tight. The buzz in his fingers sharpened to a more honest annoyance. The third note showed up like an unwelcome auditor and stood at his shoulder.\n\n“Two,” she said.\n\nHe felt his chest open the wrong way, the door wanting to throw itself wide. He held it on the hinge.\n\n“Stop,” she said.\n\nHe stopped. The warmth cooled against his will and also because he wanted it to. The itch to pour walked up his jaw and tried to make him clench; he breathed it into a corner and left it there.\n\n“Again,” she said.\n\nThey did it once more, and once more, until the call and the feel met like clap and echo. Until stopping felt like the end of a sentence rather than a denial.\n\nHe took his hands back and flexed. The filament hadn’t moved; the buzz had. He smiled, small and real. “Better.”\n\n“Good,” she said. She put oil on her thumb again and didn’t bother to hide the wince this time. “I hate this part.”\n\n“The oil?” he asked.\n\n“The admitting,” she said. “That there’s a part I can’t precision my way past. That I use my hands like a tool I don’t want replaced and the tool lets me know it wants replacing.”\n\n“They’re your hands,” he said. “They’ll look different. They’ll still obey.”\n\n“Useful,” she said, and the word meant more than praise.\n\nThey went back to letters because letters were a way to rest without lying down. He wrote a little note in the margin next to **Stop**: **if the hum turns you into a rope, cut it**. He wasn’t sure if any man would understand that sentence. He knew she would.\n\nThe room slowed. The bell lied about rest and they didn’t answer, but they let the bell take some of the weight of deciding. The lantern threw a shape on the table that made their two shadows meet and pass on the page.\n\n“Let me see your wrist,” she said, and he gave it. She set her fingertips near, not touching, reading the mark the way a glassworker reads a ripple. “Three,” she said, to the line, as if it could be bargained with by being named, and then, quieter, “No farther.”\n\nHe heard the sentence like a clamp taking hold. “Copy.”\n\nHe should have stood then. He didn’t, because the room had begun to feel like a safe angle. He looked at her hands in the mean light—ink smudge, oil shine, tendons standing like cable under light skin—and at the way she reached to cap the bottle and the thumb caught, and the tiny exhale she didn’t want noticed.\n\n“You don’t have to hold everything,” he said back to her, to prove he’d learned English in this new dialect.\n\n“That’s what you’re here for,” she said, the corners of her mouth bending toward something not quite allowed.\n\nHe moved his hand. Not to touch her first. To put it near enough that she could decide to make the span into a join. She looked at the offered place, precise as a surveyor, and made the measurement.\n\nThis was not public work. No audience. No performance. The kiss was a small mark drawn clean, not generous, set where it would carry the most for the least spend. The tremor in his fingers steadied to nothing for the space of it. The cold at her thumb let go one degree. It made a sound in his chest like a hanger seating. It sat.\n\nThey didn’t chase it. They did what they’d been teaching all day. They stopped while it could still be read.\n\nHe breathed. The city breathed back, almost in time. Outside, someone laughed two streets away and then turned the laugh into a direction for a crew. A draft found the door and filed dust off the floor-map’s ghost.\n\n“Tomorrow,” she said, forehead resting for one second against his, a practicality, not drama, like checking a level. “We take these pages to the gate office.”\n\n“Make pictures loud,” he said.\n\n“And if the proof-master sneers,” she said, “you will not pour just to wipe the expression off his face.”\n\nHe snorted. “We’ll invite.”\n\n“We’ll invite,” she echoed.\n\nShe stood. He stood. The buzz tried to return and found fewer places to live. He rolled his sleeve down over the neat, unarguable line and felt the word **stop** attach to it like a label that might hold.\n\nThey lay down within reach of tools, a brace of bodies set where load wanted them. When the bell lied, they pretended belief. When the city asked again—and it would—they would answer. Not with a pour. With a page, a mark, a breath, a hand placed where it did the most for the least. And, when they forgot, they would remind each other where to stop.\n\n---\n\n### CHAPTER 13: MORNING-OF\n\nMorning watch put a crowd on the rib whether anyone had invited it or not.\n\nHeat-Rib 3 was a black line against a pale sky; ropes already tied where men pretended they wouldn’t fall; bells hung head-high with chalk under them: eyes, hands, back. The captain in leathers stalked the catwalk with her usual tray-balanced authority. Bram marked a small circle of work with his body. Runners moved like bright nerves along the path they’d set yesterday. Two hundred people breathed in one slow, expectant lung.\n\nTiffani flexed her fingers and the joints did not answer. Oil had stopped being persuasion; it was theater. Blue-silver did not spark at her knuckles; stiffness held, cold and precise. She could lay marks. She could teach. She could not guarantee the small nudges that made a hanger give up its last rattle and call the seam true. Not at this watch. Not with hands that had decided to argue with her.\n\nJhace took the chalk without her asking. He laid X-in-circle on the rail for practice—clean enough—and the double-bar beside it. His right hand buzzed a mild buzz he refused to dignify. The copper-brown line inside his wrist sat a finger-joint long, neat as law. It had not lengthened in the night. For now that counted.\n\nThe hum along the rib was steady as a held breath. The third note, quieter, still tapped a patient finger on the bones of anyone honest enough to admit it.\n\n“Crowd’s bigger than we wanted,” Bram said.\n\n“We wanted none,” Tiffani said.\n\n“You got a city,” the captain said, overhearing, not apologizing. “And a wall that listens when you talk. Teach fast.”\n\nTiffani looked at the hinge they’d chosen for demonstration. Honest hinge. Willing to be taught in public. She looked at her hands and said the number they were pretending not to be: “Not enough.”\n\nJhace heard it without the word. He set the chalk down and came to stand where they could talk in plain air that would not be recorded by anyone’s ledger. Close, a half-hand off. “I can carry your line,” he said. “You set the angle. I hold. You don’t have to do the whole thing.”\n\n“Wholeness isn’t Form,” she said, precise even with fear. “Your warmth invites. My work refuses. They don’t stack. Not safely.”\n\n“Then I’ll reinforce under your refusal,” he said. “A coin of warmth under the edge you’re turning. Not more. You make true. I keep it from slipping while you make it.”\n\n“Cross-spend could pull you sideways,” she said. “You just learned to stop.”\n\n“Then we stop when you say.” He was calm in the way steel is calm—because it has already chosen not to move. “If it costs me, it costs me. I’m not letting you stand in front of two hundred people with hands that won’t do the thing they think they’re here to see you do.”\n\n“That’s not how precision works,” she said.\n\n“It is how partnership does,” he said.\n\nShe did the math. Hands: compromised. Crowd: volatile. Hinge: public. His vein-mark: unforgiving. The page on **Stopping** pinned to the wall in her head: three cues—her call, his feel, the line. She needed him under her work like a brace she could trust not to wander.\n\n“It might kill you,” she said, because honesty was a tool she used even when she hated its weight.\n\n“Then we die together,” he said. He didn’t make it soft. He made it exact.\n\nShe flinched—small, involuntary—at the sentence that fit and could not be allowed to become a habit. She set a palm flat to his chest, not for tenderness—measurement. His breath under her hand was steady enough to build on. She put her forehead to his and made them share a line.\n\n“Breathe,” she said.\n\nThey matched breaths until the count stopped feeling like control and started feeling like rhythm. Forehead to forehead, weight matched, two structures aligned by choice. His tremor smoothed to a whisper the bones could ignore. The ache in her thumb shifted from glass-cold to merely difficult. The world around them decided to wait without being told to.\n\n“Say it,” he said, quiet as chalk on slate.\n\nShe had never wasted language. She didn’t start now. “Whatever happens,” she said, voice clean and unslanted, “I love you.”\n\nHe didn’t reach for metaphor or banter. He didn’t make it grand. He set his mouth near her temple like you set a tool back in its place after use and said, “I know. Me too.”\n\nThey let their foreheads rest one more breath, then separated to where work lived.\n\nBram took the rope that made their circle a fact. “Coins,” he reminded, for both of them.\n\n“Coins,” Jhace said, and looked to her.\n\n“On my breath,” Tiffani said. “You stop when I say even if your bones argue.”\n\n“Copy.”\n\nThey stepped into the small space the captain had kept clear and let two hundred people become wall. Tiffani laid chalk marks on the hinge: arrow where alignment started, square for **align first**, small note under the bell marked *hands*. She lifted her ruined, excellent hand and made the angle in the air as much with her body as with her fingers.\n\n“Lift,” she said to the crew. “Not pry. On three.” She didn’t count. She breathed once and Jhace took that as one; he fed the smallest coin of warmth under the edge she was refusing into true—just enough to make the metal consider agreeing.\n\nHer thumb seized at the worst moment. He felt the slip begin and set a second coin under it without waiting for pride to comment. She hated the help and took it. “Now,” she said, and Bram quarter-turned while the hum was willing. The hinge gave a finger of truth.\n\n“Stop,” she said immediately.\n\nHe stopped. The coin cooled where he told it to. The third note pushed. He didn’t let it write.\n\nAnother breath. “Again,” she said.\n\nThey did it. Coins, not pours. Under her line, not over it. Twice more, with the crowd learning by watching that the work was not a trick but an obedience to small instructions.\n\nShe felt it when the hinge had taken enough. “Mark,” she said, and a wallman drew X-in-circle, then the double-bar, thick as vow, hands steadier for having had a job.\n\nThe captain grinned a thin wire. “Good,” she said. “Again down-line for the fools too far to see it the first time.”\n\n“Two spans,” Tiffani said, already measuring the next. “Not sermons.”\n\nJhace rolled his sleeve to check truth. The line on his wrist had not lengthened. He tugged the fabric down quick so the part of his brain that made bargains wouldn’t try to make one now.\n\nThey stood together—close enough to borrow balance without stealing it—and turned as one to face the rib proper. The hum under Heat-Rib 3 shifted half a breath, as if the city had leaned forward to listen. Flags cracked loose from themselves and remembered they were supposed to say things. A bell found the pattern that meant *eyes*.\n\n“Ready?” Bram asked, which was ridiculous and necessary.\n\n“Ready,” Tiffani said.\n\n“Ready,” Jhace answered.\n\nThey stepped toward the demonstration line and two hundred people followed the motion with their attention. The morning had chosen. The wall would learn. Or they would. They didn’t look back. They faced Heat-Rib 3.\n\n---\n\n### CHAPTER 14: WEIGHT TEST\n\nMorning watch put a thousand small intentions on Heat-Rib 3. Ropes already tied. Bells hung head-high with chalk under them: eyes, hands, back. The captain walked the catwalk like a level held true. Two hundred people breathed together and made the steel listen.\n\nTiffani flexed. The fingers didn’t. Oil had become theater; the joint at the base of her left thumb held a cold argument and refused to be persuaded by anything but rest she wasn’t going to give it. Blue-silver stayed quiet at her knuckles. She could lay marks. She could speak the work. She could not promise the tiny, exact nudges that made a hanger confess its last rattle. Not at this watch.\n\nJhace set himself in her periphery like a brace that moved when she did. He checked the bell-notes under the signs—eyes, hands, back—touched the chalk, rolled his wrist under the sleeve like a man confirming a truth he’d decided not to fight. His right hand buzzed a small, irritating hum. The copper-brown line at his inner wrist sat finger-joint long and precise. It hadn’t advanced in the night. For now that was a kind of mercy.\n\n“Crowd’s bigger than we wanted,” Bram said, voice all hinge and comfort.\n\n“We wanted none,” she said, measuring the load path with her chin. “We brought two hands of runners. It’ll be enough.”\n\n“It will if you keep them from trying to help,” the captain said, not smiling. “Teach by doing. Then do.”\n\nTiffani nodded and drew the first marks loud: X-in-circle at yesterday’s proofed join; double-bar at the hinge they’d seat under eyes; a square for **align first**; two small arrows for **invite warmth here**. The crowd leaned without stepping—a good student’s posture.\n\nHer fingers caught when she capped the ink. She pretended not to feel it. “Sequence,” she told Jhace, and he stepped close enough that the city could have called them one person without being wrong.\n\n“Your line,” he said, calm as a plate not yet asked to move. “My coin under it. On your breath. We stop when you say.”\n\n“That’s the whole plan,” she said.\n\n“It’s a good plan,” he said.\n\nHis steadiness was a measurable thing. She put the flat of her hand against his chest, set her forehead to his, and made their breathing match for three breaths. On the third, his tremor smoothed to something a body could ignore. The crowd quieted by contagion. She took her hand back and the plan was real.\n\n“Lift,” the captain called to her crew, “not pry. On her breath.” She jerked her chin at Tiffani. “You’re the metronome.”\n\nTiffani set her palm near the seam and felt the angle that wanted to exist. Precision didn’t require a perfect hand; it required a perfect refusal. She shaped refusal with her whole body—the way her shoulder sat, the way her jaw did not clench, the way her breath made a line.\n\n“One,” she said, which meant nothing mathematical and everything practical.\n\nJhace put a coin of warmth under the edge she was turning. Copper-gold rose like a held promise. The hinge considered the argument. Bram minded the quarter-turn but did not spend it.\n\n“Two,” she said, and the crew lifted, not pried. The hum shifted, a fraction truer.\n\n“Stop,” she said.\n\nHe stopped. The coin cooled because she told it to and because he wanted it to. The third note pressed his bones from the inside like a finger testing a bruise; he didn’t let it write.\n\n“Again,” she said, and the second pass seated. The hinge gave a finger of truth. A wallman drew X-in-circle with a hand steadier for having work.\n\n“Down-line,” the captain said, thin grin now, “for the fools who didn’t see the first one.”\n\nThey walked three plates and did it again, then two more. Teaching happened out loud—runners repeating the names of marks, captains translating breath to motion—without her having to shout. She felt the crowd learn: a softening across shoulders, a thousand small muscles choosing not to brace for the wrong thing.\n\nHer thumb bit when she adjusted a square a hair left. Cold ache pulsed hard enough to make her vision try to gray at the edges. She filed the pain where it belonged: noted, not obeyed. “Arrows,” she said, and handed the chalk to a kid with a bell-runner scarf who drew them like he’d been born wanting to. “Good,” she said, and meant the word like a stamp.\n\nThey reached the hinge that would not be polite.\n\nA yaw in the seat. A history of wrong quarter-turns. Load that had learned to wander. The catwalk betrayed it with a faint chatter under boots, a talkative plate somewhere that wanted to be the center of the room. The crowd felt the wrongness and leaned back without needing to be told.\n\nTiffani mapped the corrections by knuckle. “Two upstream,” she said to the captain, “one bleed down-line. Then we teach this hinge without giving it ideas.”\n\nJhace’s sleeve shifted as he rolled it—habit, ledger—then settled back down. “On your breath,” he said. “Small coins.”\n\n“Smaller than you like,” she said. It came out kinder than she felt.\n\n“Copy.”\n\nThey set. She marked **align first**—square and arrow—then laid her palm near the seam. Jhace fed a coin the size of honesty. The plate eased by a hair. Bram took a quarter when the hum was ready, not before. The hinge grudged. The chatter kept talking.\n\n“Less,” she said.\n\nHe did less. The coin barely there, exactly there. The hinge stopped sulking by the width of a hair.\n\n“Breathe,” she told the crew, and they remembered how.\n\nHer hand failed her at the worst possible moment. The base of her thumb seized and the line she was drawing went crooked in her own body. He felt it before she swore and set a second coin under her failing edge—not generous, just enough. She hated him for a breath. Then she accepted the help. Precision wasn’t loneliness. Not anymore.\n\n“Now,” she said, and Bram turned a quarter like a bell timing thunder. The hinge gave.\n\nSomething moved in the crowd—a ripple, not feet, attention tilting away. Two men with clean cuffs and patient smiles had been watching since Kindling, the particular stillness of people who believed ownership came by observation. They’d brought a scribe with a board and a quill that drank too much ink.\n\n“Make it safe, then go home,” the scribe said, loud enough to be heard. “We’ll take it from here.”\n\nThe captain didn’t turn. “No,” she said, and made the word weigh a stone.\n\n“We have lists,” the scribe said, as if that were the same as jurisdiction.\n\n“Put my name on top,” Bram said, friendly as an anvil, and the crowd laughed in a way that put the scribe outside the circle where work mattered. The men held their smiles like shields and stayed. That was fine. Tiffani did not waste precision on faces.\n\n“Again,” she said, to the hinge.\n\nThey worked it by halves until it said yes in the right language. The wallman drew the double-bar thick as a vow. Bells above them carried the grammar down-line—one for **eyes**, two for **hands**, three for **back**—and the path rearranged itself to their needs.\n\nBetween jobs, Tiffani’s fingers refused to close around the chalk. She clicked the piece to the deck and nudged it to Jhace with the back of her hand. “You draw,” she said. “I’ll call.”\n\nHe crouched, scar-shine catching pale light, and made her marks in his blocky hand—**align first**, arrows, X-in-circle—listening to her while his warmth stayed where it belonged. She watched the neat copper line inside his wrist and the way his sleeve wanted to ride up to show it. It hadn’t moved. Yet. She would keep it that way if stubbornness could be spent like coin.\n\n“Hands,” the captain called to the crowd, pointing at Tiffani’s stiff fingers so they’d see the cost and not build a superstition. “She’s paying for this same as you pay your rent. Mind your corners. Don’t make her spend where you can carry.”\n\nThe crowd nodded. Pride changed shape in their faces—less idol, more partnership. Good. That would save a life by nightfall.\n\nLate watch slanted across steel. They were two plates from the crest when the rib decided to be honest. A buckle a half-bar down—outside their demonstration circle, inside the circle of consequence—rolled its shoulder and tried to start a collapse the way men start fights when they think you won’t bother to answer.\n\n“Weight test,” the captain said, calm as clamps. “Show them.”\n\nTiffani set her palm and her stance and made refusal even with a traitor joint. “On my breath,” she told everyone, not just the crew. “Coins.”\n\nJhace set the smallest warmth under the edge she named. The buckle hesitated. Bram made a quarter-turn when the hum allowed. The buckle thought about being a story and decided against it.\n\n“Stop,” she said, and he did, instantly. The watching crews saw the stopping as the lesson, not just the making. Good. That, too, would save a life.\n\nThe men with the quill leaned together and called it luck with their mouths. The wall called it **X-in-circle** with chalk and didn’t care who heard.\n\nTiffani rolled her thumb and let the pain have one breath of acknowledgment. The ache was cold enough to make her bite her lip. She logged it. She did not let it write anything else.\n\n“Again down-line,” the captain said. “Fools in the back still think this is theater.”\n\n“It’s not,” Jhace said, without heat. “Theater has better benches.”\n\n“Less soup,” Bram said.\n\nThey went down-line and did it again, then again. By the time the crowd understood the marks and the bells and the stopping, the rib had shifted from spectacle toward habit. Habit holds. That was the point.\n\nThey stood together at the crest, where Heat-Rib 3 showed its back to the sky. The hum under it changed, not loud, not polite—like a weight picking a new place to live. Flags cracked loose from themselves and remembered their jobs. The bell over Outer Crown found a pattern Tiffani didn’t like: short, short, long, hold.\n\n“New problem,” the captain said, squinting toward West-One.\n\n“Or old problem louder,” Bram said.\n\nTiffani flexed her useless fingers and let the reality exist: she would not be the hands for the next part. She looked at Jhace. He rolled his sleeve and checked the line and put the fabric back down. They had a page for **Stopping**; they had breath; they had the grammar they had taught into the crowd. Enough to begin.\n\n“Ready?” she asked him, because ridiculous questions are also measurements.\n\n“Ready,” he said.\n\nThey turned to face the next hinge. The crowd followed the motion with their attention like metal follows a magnet. Heat-Rib 3 waited with the patience of a large animal. The bell lied about rest. They pretended to believe it for a breath, then didn’t.\n\n---\n\n### CHAPTER 15: SET TRUE\n\nThe city didn’t calm. It learned how to hold itself in public.\n\nBy middle glow, runners moved like nerves in a taught body. Bell grammar had gone from shouting to speaking. The path along Heat-Rib 3 wore rope and chalk like jewelry that meant something: *eyes*, *hands*, *back*. Crews argued less and read more. A boy with a bell scarf corrected a foreman twice his size by pointing at **double-bar** and saying, “Under load,” until the man quit pretending not to see.\n\nJhace walked the line with Tiffani at his shoulder and Bram a steady hinge behind. The crowd didn’t part so much as align around them the way metal moves for a tool it recognizes. He could feel the hum sitting truer than yesterday—still wrong in places, always wrong somewhere, but wrong with manners. The third note kept tapping his ribs, patient. It wasn’t driving. He could live with tapping.\n\n“South-Four reports two spans of marks without help,” the captain in leathers said when she found them, chalk scars on her palms, satisfaction a thin wire in her voice. “Your pictures are louder than I like. Good.”\n\n“Good is the point,” Tiffani said. Her fingers flexed once, didn’t close, took the chalk anyway. She moved like a straightedge you trusted even with a burr. The stiffness at her thumb had settled into a cold that argued with anything small. She logged that by not asking her hand for small.\n\nJhace rolled his wrist under his sleeve and looked because honesty kept him honest: the copper-brown line inside sat where it had since morning—finger-joint long, neat as law. Not farther. A cool ache lived there like a rule he’d agreed to. He pulled the sleeve down and didn’t make bargains with himself.\n\nAt the lip, a crowd had become a crew. Two wallmen from other ribs arrived to gawk and ended up hauling wedges. A quench-sister tied her hair with glass thread and took a bell rope like she was born to it. She caught Jhace’s eye and tapped her own wrist once—*watch your line*—then went back to work without ceremony. He liked her for that.\n\nThey fixed three small wrongs and taught four more people the mark for **align first**. Tiffani called angles. Jhace placed coins of warmth where her refusal needed them. Bram quarter-turned when the hum allowed. The world did not applaud; it obeyed.\n\nAt late glow they were ambushed by paperwork. The steward with ink cuffs materialized beside the rope with two bottles and hope. “Field manual,” he said, as if he had commissioned it. “We’re copying for every gate.”\n\n“You’ll copy it clean,” Tiffani said. “No new names. No pretty words. Pictures loud. Words small.”\n\nHe nodded and forgot to take a breath until he remembered how. “There’s also the matter of attribution.”\n\n“Give the city credit,” Jhace said. “It’s the only author that matters.”\n\nThe steward wrote *City* on his top page as if daring someone to argue and scuttled away like a man trying to be useful faster than his legs.\n\nThey took soup on their feet, because sitting was how you accidentally stopped working. The bowl steamed mean. Salt and bone. Good.\n\nA woman from the market—same rope on her kids as yesterday—put a loaf in Jhace’s free hand without meeting his eyes. “You didn’t pour today,” she said, tucking pride into the words. “I saw you stop because she said so.” She nodded toward Tiffani. “That’s good work.”\n\nJhace didn’t know what to do with gratitude that specific. He put it on a shelf and hoped it wouldn’t sag. “Copy,” he said, which she read as *you’re welcome* and *you’re not wrong*.\n\nBack at the hinge they’d used for morning, someone else was using it. A young crew—captain barely old enough to keep a razor honest—had set **align first** loud and were feeding coins under the edge like misers with discipline. Their wallman drew **X-in-circle** and **double-bar** thick as a vow. Jhace stopped at the rail and let the sight seat: the manual in the wild, doing the job, not needing him to say yes.\n\n“Mark’s wrong,” the young captain said to his own man, correcting himself in public. “Arrow goes left. Tiffani puts it left on this hinge. Left.” He scrubbed the chalk and redrew. Tiffani didn’t move. Her mouth allowed a fraction of a curve no one else got.\n\nTwo men with clean cuffs were there again, the ones who’d called failure “necessary.” They’d brought a scribe with a longer board and a jaw that liked lists. The scribe raised his voice without raising his courage. “Effective. But unauthorized.”\n\nThe captain in leathers ignored him. “Demonstrate the bleed,” she called to the young crew, making them teach as proof. They did, awkward and correct and then less awkward.\n\n“You’ll have trouble with them,” Bram said under his breath, nodding at the clean cuffs without giving them weight.\n\n“We’ll have trouble with someone,” Jhace said. “Might as well be men who prefer collapse tidy.”\n\nThey walked the north run, fixing simple wrongs because simple wrongs are the kind that grow teeth if you don’t. Tiffani’s chalk made grammar where language didn’t fit. Jhace set warmth in coins so small he could feel his pride complain and then quiet down. The tremor in his fingers lived at a buzz he could predict. Predictable is how you aim a life.\n\nOn the last plate before the crest, a boy about Chloen’s age with new scars and older eyes held out a cracked leather book for Tiffani to sign. It was the manual—copied by hand already, drawings clumsy and true.\n\n“Sign it?” he asked, reverent as a fool and not a fool.\n\n“Books don’t hold,” Tiffani said, but she took his stub of charcoal and wrote anyway on the inside cover, block letters like a blueprint:\n\n**SMALL COINS.  \nSTOP WHEN CALLED.  \nMARKS BEFORE PRIDE.**\n\nShe handed it back. The boy read the lines out loud and nodded like he’d just been given permission not to die.\n\nThey reached the crest at late watch, where you could see the city change its mind. Flags unrolled one by one like stubborn truths. The bell above the Crown tried a stutter and chose a sentence that meant *stand down but don’t get soft*. Runners eased against rail, grins cracked into tired faces. A woman in the second row wept like she’d been holding her breath since the Piercing and someone finally told her exhale had been invented.\n\nTiffani leaned her shoulder against the rail and let the cold in her thumb declare itself for one breath. She rubbed oil into the joint and did not hide the wince. Cost: visible on purpose. Men saw that and adjusted their ideas about heroes a degree toward useful.\n\nJhace set his forearms on the rail next to hers and looked at the line of the city. The copper-brown rule under his sleeve cooled like a thing allowed to be quiet. He had not poured. He had coins left. He had a yes ready and a stop for it to fit into. He let himself know that.\n\nBram arrived like furniture you wanted where it was. He looked pleased in the way men do when a thing they love is heavier and stays up anyway. “You two made a manual out of stubborn,” he said. “That’s a holy book if we’re being honest.”\n\n“Don’t call it holy,” Tiffani said. “It makes people stop reading.”\n\n“We’ll call it true,” Jhace said.\n\n“True holds,” Bram said, and knocked his knuckles twice against the rail to see if the universe was listening.\n\nDown-rib, bells took up an easy grammar. People began to leave in pieces, not all at once. The captain gave them her chalk stub shaved thin as a nail and said, “My crews can argue without you now.”\n\n“Good,” Tiffani said.\n\n“Bad for your heroics,” the captain said.\n\n“We hate heroics,” Jhace said, and the captain gave him the look that means *so do I* and *don’t make me prove it*.\n\nThey walked back slow because you can and should when a structure has agreed to hold for a while. The men with clean cuffs had posted a notice near the Archive steps—stamped, official, important in the way paper thinks it is. *All interventions to be filed; unauthorized methods subject to sanction.* Someone had already drawn a mustache on the seal and written **Marks before pride** underneath in tidy block letters that looked suspiciously like the boy’s.\n\n“New problem,” Bram said, cheerful as rust.\n\n“Old problem with fonts,” Tiffani said.\n\nThey reached the forge at late glow. The wall wore their pages: **Marks**, **Listening**, **Spending**, **Stopping**, **Crowd Grammar**, **Bell Codes**. Chloen slept in the bad stool like it had been built for her spine. Runners sprawled on the floor with their scarves as pillows. The door was cracked and the city’s breath came in and out like a patient learning to breathe without counting.\n\nJhace washed his hands and left them unwrapped. Scar-shine. No symmetry to the harm. Honest. He turned his wrist to look at the line because not looking wasn’t a strategy. Still three. Still cool. He touched the edge of the mark with the nail of his smallest finger, a private audit, and put his sleeve down like a promise.\n\nTiffani pinned one last page, fresh ink, still wet: **HOLD IS A HABIT.** Under it, smaller: **Teach someone else.** She stepped back until the wall agreed with her. Her hand found his forearm without having to search; didn’t cling, didn’t need to. Parallel lines at the job.\n\n“Quiet ignition,” Bram said, approving the wall again like a brace man does when a thing ends in the place he wanted. “You lit it.”\n\n“We set it where it could light itself,” Tiffani said.\n\nJhace listened to the room—breaths stacked, tools near enough to touch, soup going stubborn on the banked heat. Fear still lived in him. It had better manners now. He turned to her and didn’t bother with poetry.\n\n“We’ll keep saying stop,” he said.\n\n“You’ll keep stopping,” she said.\n\n“Copy,” he said.\n\nOutside, a bell lied about rest. They pretended to believe it long enough to make belief useful. The city would ask again in a watch, or a breath, or a finger’s width. They would answer with marks, with coins, with refusal, with the kind of love that teaches a structure to hold and then stands there to make sure it does.\n\nSet true. Then watch it. Then set it again.\n```"
          ]
        },

      "message": {

        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "This is an excellent chapter. It's a 100% perfect execution of the \"Public Demonstration\" beat, and it hits every single one of the 9 required plot points from the bible, from the crowd setup to the \"coins, not pours\" lesson, to the \"new problem\" cliffhanger.\n\nHowever, I must reject this draft. It fails the compliance check on one single, non-negotiable point.\n\nCRITICAL CANON VIOLATION: Missing Mandatory Cost\nThis chapter is 100% compliant in plot, character, lexicon, and knowledge gates, but it contains a fatal error: it fails to log a mandatory, on-page cost for Tiffani.\n\nThe Rule: The soulpulse-resonance-system-production-canon.md file is explicit. The \"Three-Line Rule\" states: \"Costs are mandatory and visible\" [cite: docs/01-canon/soulpulse-resonance-system-production-canon.md]. Section 3.1 states: \"Mandatory Costs (choose ≥1 per working on-page)\" [cite: docs/01-canon/soulpulse-resonance-system-production-canon.md].\n\nThe Violation: Tiffani actively uses her power multiple times in this chapter (e.g., \"blue-silver thin flickers,\" \"blue-silver ghosting her knuckles,\" \"Tiffani crawled the flange into line\"), but no corresponding cost (physical or psychological) is logged for her on the page.\n\nThe Precedent: In Chapters 8, 9, and 10, her cost was correctly logged as stiffness/pain in her hands (e.g., \"oiled her fingers with grudging care,\" \"oiled the base of her left thumb with a sting\"). This chapter omits this established, recurring cost.\n\nConclusion:\n\nThis is a \"don't-touch-it\" solid chapter except for this single violation. To be compliant, it must include at least one beat referencing Tiffani's cost, no matter how small.\n\nAction: Please revise to include a minor beat logging Tiffani's cost. For example:\n\nTiffani flexed her stiff fingers before answering.\n\nShe rubbed the ache in her thumb where the blue-silver had passed.\n\n...her knuckles aching with the familiar, cold stiffness of her work.\n\nOnce this beat is added, the chapter will be 100% greenlit."
          ]
        },

    "b0198dfa-b454-4251-aef7-e6aa502b2fb3": {
      "children": [
        "08dcd0bb-1c32-4d43-981a-09a965d3fd7f"
      ],
      "id": "b0198dfa-b454-4251-aef7-e6aa502b2fb3",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# Insert the Corlexi horror block after the sentence \"Hands lacked principles. He worked around them.\"\r\nfrom pathlib import Path\r\nfrom docx import Document\r\nimport re\r\n\r\nbase = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds.docx\")\r\ndoc = Document(str(base))\r\nfull = \"\\n\".join(p.text for p in doc.paragraphs)\r\n\r\n# Locate Corlexi section boundaries\r\nm_start = list(re.finditer(r\"Corlexi\\s*—\\s*Perfect\\s+Lines\", full))[-1]\r\nstart_idx = m_start.start()\r\n\r\nm_end = re.search(r\"Mobel\\s*—\\s*Cleansing\\s+Fire\", full[start_idx:])\r\nassert m_end, \"Could not find 'Mobel — Cleansing Fire' after Corlexi section.\"\r\nend_idx = start_idx + m_end.start()\r\n\r\nsection = full[start_idx:end_idx]\r\n\r\nanchor = \"Hands lacked principles. He worked around them.\"\r\ninsert_block = (\r\n    \"Meve will still be useful, he reminded himself, and looked for the fault he could remove.\\n\\n\"\r\n    \"Adren’s hands shook as he lifted the next length. The tremor traveled into the truss and made a small, ragged song.\\n\"\r\n    \"“Still,” Corlexi said. The word did not arrive as instruction. It arrived as fact.\\n\\n\"\r\n    \"Blue-silver gathered across his knuckles, thin as wire. He pressed two fingers to Adren’s forearm and drew a line the way he drew it in steel—along grain, not across it. \"\r\n    \"The boy’s tremor flattened. The arm held with a borrowed geometry that did not belong to meat.\\n\\n\"\r\n    \"Adren sucked a breath. “Master, that hurts.”\\n\\n\"\r\n    \"“Pain is a measurement,” Corlexi said. “It tells us where the slack was.”\\n\\n\"\r\n    \"Meve watched and did not speak. The lattice sang cleaner. The crew stood taller as if someone had subtracted weight from the day and added it somewhere else.\\n\\n\"\r\n    \"Adren tried to flex. The wrist did not answer like a wrist. It answered like a brace. Corlexi approved. The work would hold better now.\\n\\n\"\r\n)\r\n\r\nassert anchor in section, \"Anchor sentence not found in Corlexi section.\"\r\nsection_updated = section.replace(anchor, anchor + \"\\n\\n\" + insert_block, 1)\r\n\r\n# Rebuild full text\r\nnew_full = full[:start_idx] + section_updated + full[end_idx:]\r\n\r\n# Save DOCX and MD\r\nnew_doc = Document()\r\nfor line in new_full.split(\"\\n\"):\r\n    new_doc.add_paragraph(line)\r\n\r\nout_docx = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds-REV-InterludeI-Corlexi.docx\")\r\nnew_doc.save(str(out_docx))\r\n\r\nout_md = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds-REV-InterludeI-Corlexi.md\")\r\nout_md.write_text(new_full, encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\n\r\nprint(\"Inserted Corlexi horror passage in Interlude I.\")\r\nprint(\"Saved:\", out_docx)\r\nprint(\"Saved:\", out_md)"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1763206029.474368,
        "end_turn": false,

            "code": "# Re-run the revision with a clean session.\r\n\r\nfrom pathlib import Path\r\nimport re\r\n\r\nfrom docx import Document\r\n\r\nrev_path = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds-REVISED.docx\")\r\nassert rev_path.exists(), \"Revised DOCX not found; please ensure the previous revision file exists at /mnt/data.\"\r\n\r\ndoc = Document(str(rev_path))\r\nparas = [p.text for p in doc.paragraphs]\r\ntext = \"\\n\".join(paras)\r\n\r\nchanges = []\r\n\r\n# ---- 1) Insert Corlexi horror passage before Part B ----\r\ncorlexi_insert = (\r\n    \"Meve will still be useful, he reminded himself, and looked for the fault he could remove.\\n\\n\"\r\n    \"Adren’s hands shook as he lifted the next length. The tremor traveled into the truss and made a small, ragged song.\\n\"\r\n    \"“Still,” Corlexi said. The word did not arrive as instruction. It arrived as fact.\\n\\n\"\r\n    \"Blue-silver gathered across his knuckles, thin as wire. He pressed two fingers to Adren’s forearm and drew a line the way he drew it in steel—along grain, not across it. The boy’s tremor flattened. The arm held with a borrowed geometry that did not belong to meat.\\n\\n\"\r\n    \"Adren sucked a breath. “Master, that hurts.”\\n\\n\"\r\n    \"“Pain is a measurement,” Corlexi said. “It tells us where the slack was.”\\n\\n\"\r\n    \"Meve watched and did not speak. The lattice sang cleaner. The crew stood taller as if someone had subtracted weight from the day and added it somewhere else.\\n\\n\"\r\n    \"Adren tried to flex. The wrist did not answer like a wrist. It answered like a brace. Corlexi approved. The work would hold better now.\\n\\n\"\r\n)\r\n\r\npattern_b = r\"(PART B:\\s*MOBEL\\s*—\\s*CLEANs?ING FIRE)\"\r\nif re.search(pattern_b, text, flags=re.IGNORECASE):\r\n    text = re.sub(pattern_b, corlexi_insert + r\"\\1\", text, count=1, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\r\n    changes.append(\"Inserted Corlexi body-horror passage before PART B.\")\r\n\r\n# ---- 2) Fix grounding lines for 7-10 ----\r\nreplacements = [\r\n    (r\"(Chapter 7:[^\\n]*\\n)(Two hundred bodies and bad air pressed at street level\\.)\",\r\n     r\"\\1Their breath made small clouds in the morning cold.\"),\r\n    (r\"(Chapter 8:[^\\n]*\\n)(Ink and hot metal gave the forge a bitter, steady smell\\.)\",\r\n     r\"\\1Two hundred bodies made the rib warmer than the bells said it should be.\"),\r\n    (r\"(Chapter 9:[^\\n]*\\n)(The ink office was warmer than the bells said it should be\\.)\",\r\n     r\"\\1Ink stained his fingers the color of old bruises.\"),\r\n    (r\"(Chapter 10:[^\\n]*\\n)(Night-shift cold moved in the joints\\.)\",\r\n     r\"\\1Night-shift cold made his scars ache.\")\r\n]\r\n\r\nfor pat, rep in replacements:\r\n    new_text, n = re.subn(pat, rep, text, flags=re.DOTALL)\r\n    if n:\r\n        text = new_text\r\n        changes.append(f\"Adjusted grounding line with pattern: {pat[:30]}...\")\r\n\r\n# Ensure Chapter 9 ink-grounding exists\r\nif not re.search(r\"Chapter 9:[^\\n]*\\n.*Ink stained his fingers the color of old bruises\\.\", text, flags=re.DOTALL):\r\n    text = re.sub(r\"(Chapter 9:[^\\n]*\\n)\", r\"\\1Ink stained his fingers the color of old bruises.\\n\\n\", text, count=1, flags=re.DOTALL)\r\n    changes.append(\"Inserted missing Chapter 9 grounding line at opening.\")\r\n\r\n# ---- Write DOCX and MD ----\r\nfrom docx import Document as Doc2\r\nnew_doc = Doc2()\r\nfor line in text.split(\"\\n\"):\r\n    new_doc.add_paragraph(line)\r\nout_path = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds-REVISED2.docx\")\r\nnew_doc.save(str(out_path))\r\n\r\nmd_out = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds-REVISED2.md\")\r\nmd_out.write_text(text, encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\n\r\nprint(\"Applied changes:\")\r\nfor c in changes:\r\n    print(\"-\", c)\r\nprint(\"Saved:\", out_path)\r\nprint(\"Saved:\", md_out)",
