Source.file\nTitle: MEASUREMENT-LEXICON-COMPLETE.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file15\nContent Snippet:\n---\n\n## Usage in Prose\n\n- \"Heat the bar to cherry before striking.\"\n- \"The glass glowed lemon-bright in the glory-hole.\"\n- \"At blood heat, the metal becomes workable.\"\n- \"Don't touch—it's still breath-steam hot.\"\n- \"The pour reached blinding white, too bright to watch.\"\n\n---\n\n# SECTION 6: PRESSURE SYSTEM\n\n## Exponential Scale (Craft-Based)\n\n| Unit | Approximate Real-World | Description | Usage |\n|------|----------------------|-------------|-------|\n| **Touch** | <1 psi (finger pressure) | Barely felt | Sensory threshold |\n| **Grip** | ~5 psi (hand squeeze) | Firm hold | Tool manipulation |\n| **Clamp** | ~25 psi (vice pressure) | Secure hold | Workshop work |\n| **Head** | ~14.7 psi (1 atm) | Standard pressure | Atmosphere/water column |\n| **Lift** | ~147 psi (10 atm) | High pressure | Steam systems |\n| **Burst** | ~735 psi (50 atm) | Rupture point | Failure threshold |\n| **Forge-blow** | ~3,675 psi (250 atm) | Extreme force | Industrial equipment |\n\n---\n\n## Usage in Prose\n\n- \"The riser held three heads of pressure.\"\n- \"Release-vents opened at burst threshold.\"\n- \"The heat-grid ran at two-lift pressure during peak.\"\n- \"He tightened the clamp until the joint held firm.\"\n\n---\n\n# SECTION 7: TIME INCREMENTS (SUB-TIDE)\n\n## Within-Day Measurement (Smaller Than Tide)\n\n| Unit | Real-World Equivalent | Description | Usage |\n|------|----------------------|-------------|-------|\n| **Breath** | ~3 seconds (one cycle) | Single breath | Immediate timing |\n| **Stroke** | ~6 seconds (hammer blow) | Tool operation | Work rhythm |\n| **Set** | ~30 seconds (5 strokes) | Work sequence | Short tasks |\n| **Spell** | ~2.5 minutes (5 sets) | Brief duration | Task completion |\n| **Watch** | ~12.5 minutes (5 spells) | Attention span | Shift breaks |\n| **Turn** | ~1 hour (5 watches) | Work period | Major task blocks |\n| **Shift** | ~5 hours (5 turns) | Daily work period | One tide-period |\n\n---\n\n## Usage in Prose\n\n- \"Hold for eight breaths before releasing.\"\n- \"Three strokes to set the rivet.\"\n- \"Give me two sets to finish.\"\n- \"The weld takes a full spell to cool.\"\n- \"They worked through a turn without stopping.\"\n- \"He's been at it for two watches straight.\"\n- \"The morning shift ends at Shaping.\"\n\n---\n\n## Quick Conversions\n\n**1 Tide = 5 Shifts = 25 Turns = 125 Watches = 625 Spells = 3,125 Sets = 15,625 Strokes = 31,250 Breaths**\n\n---\n\n# SECTION 8: PRECISION / TOLERANCE SYSTEM\n\n## Measurement Accuracy (Engineering Precision)\n\n| Term | Approximate Real-World | Description | Usage |\n|------|----------------------|-------------|-------|\n| **Eye-true** | ±5mm | Visual accuracy | Rough work |\n| **Finger-true** | ±1mm | Touch verification | Craft work |\n| **Scribe-true** | ±0.1mm | Marked precision | Fine work |\n| **Hair-true** | ±0.01mm | Extreme precision | Master work |\n| **Vein-true** | Perfect | Soulpulse-guided | Impossible precision |\n\n---\n\n## Usage in Prose\n# 【16†MEASUREMENT-LEXICON-COMPLETE.md†file-PFswYdxKE6YwpK54Eg1i9q】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: MEASUREMENT-LEXICON-COMPLETE.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file16\nContent Snippet:\n## Pressure Idioms\n- \"Under three heads\" (moderate stress)\n- \"At burst point\" (breaking under pressure)\n- \"Lift-heavy tension\" (extreme pressure)\n\n---\n\n# SECTION 15: SUBSTITUTION TABLE (FORBIDDEN TERMS)\n\n## Distance — DO NOT USE\n\n| Forbidden | Use Instead |\n|-----------|-------------|\n| centimeter, cm | finger |\n| meter, m | bar, stride |\n| kilometer, km | circuit, reach |\n| mile | reach, expanse |\n| inch | finger, hand |\n| foot | span, forearm |\n| yard | stride, bar |\n\n## Weight — DO NOT USE\n\n| Forbidden | Use Instead |\n|-----------|-------------|\n| gram, g | grain, pinch |\n| kilogram, kg | stone |\n| pound, lb | stone |\n| ton, tonne | ingot, pour |\n| ounce, oz | handful |\n\n## Volume — DO NOT USE\n\n| Forbidden | Use Instead |\n|-----------|-------------|\n| milliliter, ml | drop, thimble |\n| liter, L | cup, jar |\n| gallon | cask |\n| barrel | cask, vat |\n\n## Temperature — DO NOT USE\n\n| Forbidden | Use Instead |\n|-----------|-------------|\n| degrees, Celsius, Fahrenheit | cherry, lemon, white |\n| hot, warm, cold (numeric) | blood heat, hand-warm, chill |\n\n## Time — DO NOT USE\n\n| Forbidden | Use Instead |\n|-----------|-------------|\n| second, sec | breath, stroke |\n| minute, min | set, spell |\n| hour, hr | watch, turn |\n| day | tide |\n| week | anneal |\n| month | crucible |\n| year | casting |\n\n---\n\n# SECTION 16: MATHEMATICAL QUICK REFERENCE\n\n## Core Scaling Ratios\n\n**Time System:** Alternating ×5 and ×7 scaling  \n**Distance System:** Alternating ×2 and ×5 scaling  \n**Weight System:** Consistent ×50 scaling  \n**Volume System:** Consistent ×50 scaling  \n**Pressure System:** ×5 and ×10 scaling  \n**Speed System:** Doubling pattern  \n\n---\n\n## Conversion Cheat Sheet\n\n### Distance\n- 1 Bar = 4 Strides = 180 Fingers\n- 1 Rod = 5 Bars = 900 Fingers\n- 1 Circuit = 5 Vein-spans = ~1 km\n\n### Weight\n- 1 Stone = 50 Handfuls = 2,500 Pinches\n- 1 Load = 50 Stone = 2,500 Handfuls\n- 1 Ingot = 50 Loads = 2,500 Stone\n\n### Volume\n- 1 Jar = 50 Cups = 2,500 Thimbles\n- 1 Cask = 50 Jars = 2,500 Cups\n- 1 Vat = 50 Casks = 2,500 Jars\n\n### Time (Sub-Tide)\n- 1 Turn = 5 Watches = 25 Spells\n- 1 Shift = 5 Turns = 125 Spells\n- 1 Tide = 5 Shifts = 625 Spells\n\n---\n\n# SECTION 17: WRITER'S IMPLEMENTATION CHECKLIST\n\nBefore writing each chapter:\n- [ ] Replace all metric/imperial units with Te'Oga terms\n- [ ] Use observational temperature (cherry, lemon) not numbers\n- [ ] Describe time with breaths/watches/tides, not seconds/hours\n- [ ] Use exponential units appropriately (don't jump from finger to reach)\n- [ ] Maintain cultural voice (craft-based, not scientific)\n# 【17†MEASUREMENT-CONVERSION-CHART.md†file-G36trJPXAYqsLbumKvExEj】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: MEASUREMENT-CONVERSION-CHART.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file17\nContent Snippet:\n---\n\n# WEIGHT CONVERSIONS\n\n## Metric → Te'Oga\n\n| If You're Thinking... | Write Instead... | Ratio |\n|----------------------|------------------|-------|\n| 0.05 g | grains | 1 grain ≈ 0.065g |\n| 3 g | pinches | 1 pinch ≈ 3g |\n| 150 g | handfuls | 1 handful ≈ 150g |\n| 7-8 kg | stone | 1 stone ≈ 7.5kg |\n| 375 kg | loads | 1 load ≈ 375kg |\n| 18-19 tonnes | ingots | 1 ingot ≈ 18.75 tonnes |\n| 900+ tonnes | pours | 1 pour ≈ 937 tonnes |\n\n---\n\n## Imperial → Te'Oga\n\n| If You're Thinking... | Write Instead... | Ratio |\n|----------------------|------------------|-------|\n| 1 oz | 5-6 handfuls | 1 oz ≈ 28g ≈ 5-6 handfuls |\n| 16 lbs | 1 stone | 16 lbs ≈ 7.5kg ≈ 1 stone |\n| 825 lbs | 1 load | 825 lbs ≈ 375kg ≈ 1 load |\n| 20 tons | 1 ingot | 20 tons ≈ 18.75 tonnes ≈ 1 ingot |\n\n---\n\n# TIME CONVERSIONS\n\n## Seconds/Minutes/Hours → Te'Oga\n\n| If You're Thinking... | Write Instead... | Ratio |\n|----------------------|------------------|-------|\n| 3 seconds | 1 breath | 1 breath ≈ 3 sec |\n| 6 seconds | 1 stroke | 1 stroke ≈ 6 sec |\n| 30 seconds | 1 set | 1 set ≈ 30 sec |\n| 2.5 minutes | 1 spell | 1 spell ≈ 2.5 min |\n| 12.5 minutes | 1 watch | 1 watch ≈ 12.5 min |\n| 1 hour | 1 turn | 1 turn ≈ 1 hour |\n| 5 hours | 1 shift | 1 shift ≈ 5 hours |\n| 24 hours | 1 tide | 1 tide ≈ 1 day |\n| 1 week | 1 anneal | 1 anneal ≈ 5 days |\n| 1 month | 1 crucible | 1 crucible ≈ 35 days |\n| 1 year | 1 casting | 1 casting ≈ 315 days |\n| 10 years | 1 smelting | 1 smelting ≈ 10 years |\n| 100 years | 1 masterwork | 1 masterwork ≈ 100 years |\n| 1000 years | 1 epoch | 1 epoch ≈ 1000 years |\n\n---\n\n# VOLUME CONVERSIONS\n\n## Metric → Te'Oga\n\n| If You're Thinking... | Write Instead... | Ratio |\n|----------------------|------------------|-------|\n| 0.05 ml | drops | 1 drop ≈ 0.05ml |\n| 2.5 ml | thimbles | 1 thimble ≈ 2.5ml |\n| 125 ml | cups | 1 cup ≈ 125ml |\n| 6 L | jars | 1 jar ≈ 6.25L |\n| 300 L | casks | 1 cask ≈ 312L |\n| 15,000 L | vats | 1 vat ≈ 15,600L |\n| 780,000 L | pools | 1 pool ≈ 780KL |\n\n---\n\n## Imperial → Te'Oga\n# 【18†MEASUREMENT-CONVERSION-CHART.md†file-G36trJPXAYqsLbumKvExEj】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: MEASUREMENT-CONVERSION-CHART.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file18\nContent Snippet:\n---\n\n# SPEED CONVERSIONS\n\n| If You're Thinking... | Write Instead... | Context |\n|----------------------|------------------|---------|\n| 1 km/h | creep | Slow walk |\n| 5 km/h | walk | Normal pace |\n| 10 km/h | trot | Brisk pace |\n| 15 km/h | run | Sprint |\n| 30 km/h | gallop | Horse speed |\n| 60 km/h | rush | Fast cart |\n\n---\n\n# PRESSURE CONVERSIONS\n\n| If You're Thinking... | Write Instead... | Context |\n|----------------------|------------------|---------|\n| <1 psi | touch | Finger pressure |\n| 5 psi | grip | Hand squeeze |\n| 25 psi | clamp | Vice pressure |\n| 14.7 psi (1 atm) | head | Standard pressure |\n| 147 psi (10 atm) | lift | High pressure |\n| 735 psi (50 atm) | burst | Rupture point |\n\n---\n\n# COMMON TRANSLATION EXAMPLES\n\n## Example 1: Distance\n**Thinking:** \"The beam is 3.6 meters long.\"  \n**Write:** \"The beam measured two bars.\"\n\n## Example 2: Weight\n**Thinking:** \"It weighs about 15 kilograms.\"  \n**Write:** \"It weighed two stone.\"\n\n## Example 3: Time\n**Thinking:** \"Wait 15 seconds.\"  \n**Write:** \"Hold for five breaths.\"\n\n## Example 4: Temperature\n**Thinking:** \"Heat it to 675°C.\"  \n**Write:** \"Heat it to cherry.\"\n\n## Example 5: Volume\n**Thinking:** \"Add 5 milliliters.\"  \n**Write:** \"Add two thimbles.\"\n\n## Example 6: Multiple Units\n**Thinking:** \"The crack is 6cm deep, the metal is at 1100°C, wait 3 minutes.\"  \n**Write:** \"The crack ran three fingers deep. Heat to lemon. Hold for a spell before striking.\"\n\n---\n\n# NATURAL COMBINING PATTERNS\n\n## Distance Combinations\n- \"a stride and two spans\"\n- \"three fingers shy of a hand\"\n- \"two bars and a forearm\"\n- \"half a rod or so\"\n\n## Weight Combinations\n- \"ten stone, maybe twelve\"\n- \"a load and three-quarters\"\n- \"two handfuls and a pinch\"\n\n## Time Combinations\n- \"two turns and a watch\"\n- \"five breaths, no more\"\n- \"a spell and three sets\"\n- \"before the tide turns\"\n\n---\n\n# DRAFTING WORKFLOW\n\n## Step 1: Write Naturally\nDraft using whatever units feel natural to you (metric, imperial, whatever).\n\n## Step 2: Search & Mark\nSearch for forbidden terms: `cm|meter|km|second|minute|hour|gram|kg|degree`\n\n## Step 3: Replace Using This Chart\nLook up each marked term in the appropriate conversion table above.\n\n## Step 4: Naturalize\nRead aloud—does it sound like a Te'Ogan craftsperson would say it?\n\n## Step 5: Verify\nCheck that precision level matches character expertise (eye-true vs. hair-true).\n\n---\n\n# MEMORY AIDS\n\n## The Five-Pattern\n- **Time:** 5 tides = anneal, 5 anneals = ??? (no, 7 = crucible)\n- **Distance:** Mostly doubles (hand→span) or quintuples (bar→rod)\n- **Weight:** Always ×50 (pinch→handful→stone→load)\n- **Volume:** Always ×50 (drop→thimble→cup→jar)\n# 【19†MEASUREMENT-CONVERSION-CHART.md†file-G36trJPXAYqsLbumKvExEj】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: MEASUREMENT-CONVERSION-CHART.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file19\nContent Snippet:\n# MEASUREMENT CONVERSION VISUAL GUIDE\n## Real-World ↔ Te'Oga Translation Chart\n\n---\n\n## HOW TO USE THIS GUIDE\n\nWhen drafting and you think in real-world units, use this chart to instantly translate to Te'Oga terms.\n\n---\n\n# DISTANCE CONVERSIONS\n\n## Metric → Te'Oga\n\n| If You're Thinking... | Write Instead... | Ratio |\n|----------------------|------------------|-------|\n| 1-3 cm | fingers | 1 finger ≈ 2cm |\n| 5-15 cm | hands | 1 hand ≈ 10cm |\n| 20-30 cm | spans | 1 span ≈ 23cm |\n| 40-50 cm | forearms | 1 forearm ≈ 45cm |\n| 80-100 cm | strides | 1 stride ≈ 90cm |\n| 1.5-2 m | bars | 1 bar ≈ 1.8m |\n| 8-10 m | rods | 1 rod ≈ 9m |\n| 40-50 m | cables | 1 cable ≈ 45m |\n| 200-250 m | vein-spans | 1 vein-span ≈ 225m |\n| 1 km | circuits | 1 circuit ≈ 1.1km |\n| 5 km | grids | 1 grid ≈ 5.5km |\n| 25 km | reaches | 1 reach ≈ 27.5km |\n| 100-150 km | expanses | 1 expanse ≈ 137km |\n\n---\n\n## Imperial → Te'Oga\n\n| If You're Thinking... | Write Instead... | Ratio |\n|----------------------|------------------|-------|\n| 1 inch | 1-2 fingers | 1 inch ≈ 1.3 fingers |\n| 4 inches | 1 hand | 4 inches ≈ 10cm ≈ 1 hand |\n| 9 inches | 1 span | 9 inches ≈ 23cm ≈ 1 span |\n| 18 inches | 2 spans or 1 forearm | 18 inches ≈ 45cm |\n| 1 yard | 1 bar | 1 yard ≈ 0.9m ≈ half a bar |\n| 6 feet | 1 bar | 6 feet ≈ 1.8m ≈ 1 bar |\n| 30 feet | 1 rod | 30 feet ≈ 9m ≈ 1 rod |\n| 150 feet | 1 cable | 150 feet ≈ 45m ≈ 1 cable |\n| 750 feet | 1 vein-span | 750 feet ≈ 225m ≈ 1 vein-span |\n| 0.7 miles | 1 circuit | 0.7 miles ≈ 1.1km ≈ 1 circuit |\n| 3.5 miles | 1 grid | 3.5 miles ≈ 5.5km ≈ 1 grid |\n| 17 miles | 1 reach | 17 miles ≈ 27.5km ≈ 1 reach |\n| 85 miles | 1 expanse | 85 miles ≈ 137km ≈ 1 expanse |\n\n---\n\n# WEIGHT CONVERSIONS\n\n## Metric → Te'Oga\nVisible:     "d1f64de4-d6fd-4877-9908-83a9baaeec2e": {

        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "Alright, let’s wire the whole damn thing together.\n\nYou already have a **plot bible** and a **people bible**. What you’re asking now is:\n\n> “How do I break N01 into beats **and** use conversations as *motors* instead of just flavor text so the story feels character-driven, not just event-driven?”\n\nLet’s do exactly that.\n\n---\n\n## 0. Big Picture: N01 in Four Movements\n\n**Act I – Breach & First Bond (Ch1–5 + Interlude I)**  \nWorld is stable → Piercing hits → Jhace & Tiffani meet → first magic use → first cost.\n\n**Act II – Building Grammar & Partnership (Ch6–10)**  \nThey learn each other, invent a shared language (marks, field manual, rhythm), and decide to scale it.\n\n**Act III – Fracture Edge (Ch11 + Interlude II)**  \nJhace overreaches, brushes Hollowing; Tiffani saves him; they almost lose everything.\n\n**Act IV – Commit to Scale (Ch12–15)**  \nFallout, confession, public demonstration, then a quieter but heavier resolution: “We’re doing this for the rest of our lives.”\n\nConversations sit **at the edges of these movements** and **around key choices**, nudging plot into motion **from the inside of the characters**, not the other way around.\n\n---\n\n## 1. Act I – Breach & First Bond (Ch1–5 + Interlude I)\n\n### CH1 – Jhace: “Something Wrong”\n\n- **External beat:**  \n  Routine grid inspection → subtle wrongness → Piercing begins → “Something massive was descending through the bones of the world.”\n\n- **Internal/relationship beat:**  \n  - Jhace’s **hyperresponsibility + metal-as-truth** established.  \n  - Bram as father-figure (“Your parents didn’t leave you. They died.”)  \n  - Chloen as eager apprentice.\n\n- **Dialogue function:**  \n  - Bram’s “Metal doesn’t leave” convo **motivates** Jhace’s later refusal to step back from risk.  \n  - Chloen’s questions show he’s already **teaching as love language**.\n\n**Character-driven payoff later:**  \nWhen Jhace refuses to walk away in Ch5 & Ch11, it isn’t random “heroism.” We have this chapter’s conversations as **setup for his flaw/virtue**: he equates stepping away with betrayal.\n\n---\n\n### CH2 – Tiffani: “Warped Horizon”\n\n- **External beat:**  \n  Tiffani measures the horizon → detects non-local deflection → world bending → first roar from the Piercing.\n\n- **Internal/relationship beat:**  \n  - Precision + isolation.  \n  - People = noise; numbers = refuge.  \n  - Marisse hinted as old wound.\n\n- **Dialogue function:**  \n  - Her answers to Mikel (“Systematic means it is real. Solving comes after knowing.”) define her **ethic**.  \n  - When she later chooses **messy action over clean measurement**, it lands as character growth, not convenience.\n\n**Character-driven payoff later:**  \nIn Ch11 when she tackles Jhace mid-Hollowing, she’s literally betraying her own “precision-first, keep distance” ethic we established here. That decision *means* something because we heard her talk this way first.\n\n---\n\n### CH3 – Jhace: “Load Path”\n\n- **External beat:**  \n  Underface crisis, purge sequence, handling pressure, third note introduced, topside to see bulge.\n\n- **Internal/relationship beat:**  \n  - Jhace as **tactical leader**, not show-off. “No hero work.”  \n  - First physical reaction to the third note (his pulse tripping) → foreshadowing Soulpulse sensitivity.\n\n- **Dialogue function:**  \n  - “Red hash means fix-order… Today we make new marks.” → establishes **mark-grammar** as language.  \n  - “Your marks are still your marks. Don’t let the wrongness tell you stories.” → Bram reinforcing Jhace’s **trust in his own judgment**.\n\n**Character-driven payoff later:**  \n- The whole **field manual / public demonstration arc** grows from this: chalk marks as a **shared language** start here in casual talk.  \n- Jhace’s later shame/doubt about his overspend in Ch11 hits harder because we saw how sacred his marks are to him.\n\n---\n\n### CH4 – “Glass, Hands”\n\n- **External beat:**  \n  Glass-house crisis → molten glass rescue → Jhace burns his hands → Tiffani saved → Chloen injured.\n\n- **Internal/relationship beat:**  \n  - Jhace chooses pain over letting Tiffani die.  \n  - Tiffani sees someone match her **risk tolerance and competence**.  \n  - Bram + Chloen: mini family unit under strain.\n\n- **Dialogue function:**  \n  - Tiffani’s coaching mid-crisis (“Draw, don’t scoop. Thin it. Keep it singing.”) is **how she loves**: instructive, precise.  \n  - Her “Thank you. Not big. Exact.” is the first **tiny emotional concession**.\n\n**Character-driven payoff later:**  \n- This is why she trusts him enough to **let him under her guard** in Ch6–10.  \n- This is why he feels a **debt** to her + Chloen that later pushes him into overspending in Ch11.\n\n---\n\n### CH5 – “Copper-Gold”\n\n- **External beat:**  \n  Quench-hall triage → Chloen’s wound worsening → Jhace’s first Wholeness use → vein-burn cost appears.\n\n- **Internal/relationship beat:**  \n  - Jhace crosses the line from “fix things” to “alter bodies/world” → internal sense of **new responsibility**.  \n  - Tiffani sees and **names** it as Wholeness.  \n  - Bram realizes Jhace is now playing in a bigger arena than bolts and pins.\n\n- **Dialogue function:**  \n  - The **Quench-Hall argument slot** (we sketched) can live here: Bram vs Jhace vs Tiffani about who gets to decide cost.  \n  - That conversation becomes **motivation** for Jhace’s internal logic later:\n    - “If I don’t spend, people die.”\n    - “If I do spend, I might Hollow.”\n\n**Character-driven payoff later:**  \nEverything in Ch11 (Fracture Edge) is this conversation turned up to 11. He doesn’t overspend out of nowhere; we’ve *heard* him arguing in this direction already.\n\n---\n\n### INTERLUDE I – Corlexi & Mobel\n\n- **External beat:**  \n  Off-city glimpse: Form zealot (Corlexi) and Purity zealot (Mobel) pushing toward godhood.\n\n- **Internal/relationship beat (meta):**  \n  - Shows **other possible relationships** with power: coercive, cruel, absolutist.  \n  - Frames Jhace/Tiffani’s craft-love as a **counterpoint** to this.\n\n- **Dialogue function:**  \n  - Corlexi’s cold teaching and Mobel’s doctrine give a *shadow* version of Jhace’s & Tiffani’s values:\n    - “Perfection requires sacrifice.” vs “Wholeness requires care.”\n    - “Purity requires pain.” vs “Wholeness requires cost, not punishment.”\n\n**Character-driven payoff later:**  \nEven though they never meet, readers subconsciously compare:\n\n- Jhace’s refusal to treat people as expendable vs Mobel’s willingness.  \n- Tiffani’s precision anchored in compassion vs Corlexi’s precision anchored in control.\n\nThis shapes how we *judge* the core couple’s choices.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Act II – Building Grammar & Partnership (Ch6–10)\n\n### CH6 – “Teaching Rhythm”\n\n- **External beat:**  \n  Bram’s forge becomes classroom. Tiffani teaches Jhace to refine control; he learns to spend *less* Wholeness to get the same effect.\n\n- **Internal/relationship beat:**  \n  - She starts teaching him like an equal, not a tool.  \n  - He lets her critique his gift.  \n  - They begin to **co-own** the work.\n\n- **Dialogue function:**  \n  - This is where you get lines like:\n    - “You’re too generous. Keep some for the next breath.”  \n  - Those lines become **Jhace’s inner voice** later when he chooses to ignore that advice in Ch11.  \n  - Their back-and-forth is how we see their **trust** grow and sets up the heartbreak when he doesn’t listen.\n\n---\n\n### CH7 – “Working Lines”\n\n- **External beat:**  \n  Multiple field fixes; they move as a team under city load. “Parallel lines / working lines” speech. Chloen: “You two work like you’re married.”\n\n- **Internal/relationship beat:**  \n  - Partnership upgrades from “test run” to **established rhythm**.  \n  - Bram consciously acknowledges them as a pair (at least in the craft sense).  \n  - Chloen recognizes the subtext and just blurts it.\n\n- **Dialogue function:**\n  - Bram’s “Parallel lines don’t meet. Working lines do. At the job.” is almost a **blessing**.  \n  - Chloen’s “You work like you’re married” forces them to confront the intimacy they’re pretending is “just craft.”\n\n**Character-driven payoff later:**  \nThose lines are **echoes** when:\n\n- Tiffani decides to risk her body to drag Jhace out of Hollowing (Ch11).  \n- They admit love (Ch13).  \n- They stand together publicly (Ch14–15).\n\n---\n\n### CH8 – “City Grammar”\n\n- **External beat:**  \n  They start formalizing chalk marks and field communication—turning their internal language into something teachable.\n\n- **Internal/relationship beat:**  \n  - Tiffani sees **scale**; Jhace starts to realize their system can outlive them.  \n  - Bram shifts from skeptic to “grimly proud.”\n\n- **Dialogue function:**  \n  - Great place for **Forge Night conversation slot** (we wrote this):  \n    - Debating whether teaching the city is worth the risk.  \n    - Tiffani: “If it can’t hold at scale, it’s a toy.”  \n    - Jhace: uneasy with crowds, but unwilling to hoard safety.\n\n**Character-driven payoff later:**  \n- That talk is what **motivates** the manual in Ch9 and the public demo in Ch14.  \n- They don’t wake up suddenly wanting to perform; they argued themselves into a corner and then act accordingly.\n\n---\n\n### CH9 – “Field Manual”\n\n- **External beat:**  \n  Tiffani POV. They codify marks into a manual, test it with first crews. Force multiplier: crews begin using the grammar without them.\n\n- **Internal/relationship beat:**  \n  - Tiffani as **teacher-of-teachers**, not just glass-brain.  \n  - Jhace lets his knowledge be externalized, which is a vulnerability.  \n  - Chloen gets **agency** by illustrating / adapting the manual.\n\n- **Dialogue function:**  \n  - Crew discussions show how their system lands in ordinary mouths.  \n  - Tiffani + Jhace talk about **cost control** (“coins, not pours”; “Teach me when to stop.”) → central psychological motor for Ch11.\n\n**Character-driven payoff later:**  \n- Jhace’s line “Teach me when to stop” is what makes it a *tragedy* when he ignores the limit at Fracture Edge.  \n- Chloen’s added agency here (drawings, carrying manuals) makes her **part of the system**, not just “the injured one.”\n\n---\n\n### CH10 – “Coins, Not Pours” (Public Smaller Demo / Street Work)\n\n- **External beat:**  \n  A smaller public test (not the final rib demo yet). They show the system to a local crew/captain, prove it works, and get respect/food/cred.\n\n- **Internal/relationship beat:**  \n  - Jhace sees that **small, controlled spending** can still move crowds and save people.  \n  - Tiffani sees him taking feedback on cost.  \n  - The city begins to **see them as a unit**.\n\n- **Dialogue function:**  \n  - Townsfolk & crew talk is your **mirror**:  \n    - “You two always move like that?”  \n    - “Your kitchen promises pay” → outside confirmation that their partnership matters.\n\n**Character-driven payoff later:**  \nThis gives them a **positive success reference** before Ch11 goes sideways. So when he overreaches, it isn’t coming out of pure desperation; it’s him thinking, “I can scale what worked yesterday,” and being wrong.\n\n---\n\n## 3. Act III – Fracture Edge (Ch11 + Interlude II)\n\n### CH11 – “Fracture Edge”\n\n- **External beat:**  \n  Catastrophic failure at a major structural point. Jhace attempts an impossible Confluence (holding too many things at once), hits Hollowing onset. Tiffani sees, tackles him out, slaps him back into himself. Massive vein-burn cost.\n\n- **Internal/relationship beat:**  \n  - Jhace confronts the edge of becoming something like Corlexi/Mobel: power without self.  \n  - Tiffani chooses him over the structure in a very literal way.  \n  - This is the **climax of her arc** for N01: she abandons pure precision and does something messy, risky, emotional.\n\n- **Dialogue function:**  \n  - Her lines: “You don’t have to hold everything. That’s what I’m here for.” and his blank look when he can’t find her name → pure character-driven horror.  \n  - Their post-crisis talk (even if short) sets emotional tone for Ch12–13:\n    - “Don’t do that again.”  \n    - “Can’t promise.” / “Then I’ll be there again.”\n\n**Character-driven payoff later:**  \nCh12’s intimacy and the Ch13 morning-of “I love you” scene make sense because we *saw* her watch him nearly erase himself, and choose him anyway.\n\n---\n\n### INTERLUDE II – Salee & Eries\n\n- **External beat:**  \n  Off-city view of Entropy and identity-erosion. Salee loses herself to work-voice; Eries uses Release with terrifying detachment.\n\n- **Internal/relationship beat (meta):**  \n  - Contrasts Jhace/Tiffani’s “we hold each other in place” with:\n    - Salee’s loss of self.  \n    - Eries’s willingness to let things (and people) dissolve.\n\n- **Dialogue function:**  \n  - Interlude voices act as **negative future possibilities**.  \n  - Make readers fear that Jhace/Tiffani could become this if they stop holding each other to human standards.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Act IV – Commit to Scale (Ch12–15)\n\n### CH12 – “Drift Align” (Post-Fracture Fallout & First Kiss)\n\n- **External beat:**  \n  Recovery from Hollowing scare. They regroup physically and emotionally. First kiss.\n\n- **Internal/relationship beat:**  \n  - Jhace feels **unsteady** about his own mind/body.  \n  - Tiffani both angry and terrified; she doesn’t want a martyr, she wants a partner.  \n  - Kiss = not just romance; it’s a **binding agreement** that they’re in this together.\n\n- **Dialogue function:**  \n  - Conversations here **reframe the entire goal**:\n    - Not “fix the city at any cost,” but “fix the city without losing ourselves.”  \n  - This becomes motivation for the more careful, designed public demo later.\n\n---\n\n### CH13 – “Morning-Of Confession”\n\n- **External beat:**  \n  Morning of big demonstration. Crowd gathering. Tiffani’s hands acting up; she won’t be able to do full Form work. They decide how to divide cost.\n\n- **Internal/relationship beat:**  \n  - She admits fear—not of the rib but of what happens if they prove it works.  \n  - He commits: “Then we die together.”  \n  - She says, “Whatever happens, I love you.” / He: “I know. Me too.” (or your chosen line).\n\n- **Dialogue function:**  \n  - This is the **emotional hinge**: their confession is what justifies them taking on risk in Ch14.  \n  - The forehead-to-forehead breathing sync is a physical version of “we are one system now.”\n\n**Character-driven payoff later:**  \nWhen the rib shudders in Ch14, the reader is not thinking “cool set piece;” they’re thinking “this is the thing they might die doing; they already chose it together.”\n\n---\n\n### CH14 – “Under the Rib” (Full Public Demonstration)\n\n- **External beat:**  \n  Heat-Rib 3 demonstration before a crowd. They use grammar, field manual, crews, and their powers in controlled, coin-sized spends to prove the system works.\n\n- **Internal/relationship beat:**  \n  - This is them presenting as **a partnership to the city**.  \n  - Bram backs them up in front of others.  \n  - Chloen participates, distributing manuals / helping crews, proving she’s part of the new world too.\n\n- **Dialogue function:**  \n  - Crowd commentary (“You see that?” “They moved before the rib did.”) acts as validation.  \n  - Jhace + Tiffani’s on-the-job patter shows their **maturity**: less fear, more calibration.  \n  - Post-demo exchanges like:\n    - “You spent a little hard.”  \n    - “So did you.”  \n    - “We’ll track it.”  \n    turn cost-awareness into a shared practice.\n\n---\n\n### CH15 – “The Grip That Holds” (Resolution)\n\n- **External beat:**  \n  Aftermath at the forge / by the rib / in some quiet space. City grammar is catching on. They plan next steps; no huge fireworks, just the realization that this is now their life’s work.\n\n- **Internal/relationship beat:**  \n  - Jhace checks his vein-burn: **line hasn’t moved much, but the warmth feels thinned.**  \n  - Tiffani’s stiffness is worse, but she’s still working.  \n  - Bram is old and proud and terrified: the kids are building something bigger than him.  \n  - Chloen is alive, pissed about her limits, and ready to keep going.\n\n- **Dialogue function:**  \n  - This is where you cash out those small lines:\n    - Bram: “You two just rewrote how this city stands. Try not to die doing the rest.”  \n    - Jhace: “No promises. But we’ll design our chances.”  \n    - Tiffani: “We’ll make the marks hold. That’s the work.”\n\n**Character-driven payoff:**  \nNo “and everything was fine.” Instead:  \n“We made a thing that works. It cost us. We’re still willing to pay. Together.”\n\n---\n\n## 5. How to Make It *Feel* Character-Driven, Not Plot-Driven\n\nHere’s the actual trick:\n\nWhenever a **big plot beat** is coming (or just happened), ask:\n\n1. **Whose decision actually drives this event?**\n2. **What fear, desire, or belief of theirs does it come from?**\n3. **Where do we let them *say* or *show* that belief in dialogue just before or after?**\n\nExamples:\n\n- **Jhace’s overreach (Ch11)**  \n  - Driven by: “If I don’t hold everything, people die and it’s my fault.”  \n  - Motivation shown in **Quench-hall argument** (Ch5) + **cost-control conversations** (Ch9–10).  \n  - Result: feels inevitable and tragic, not arbitrary.\n\n- **Tiffani’s tackle/rescue (Ch11)**  \n  - Driven by: “Precision isn’t worth shit if he’s gone.”  \n  - Motivation shown in Ch2 (“focus over people”), then chipped at by every shared work scene, explicitly reversed by the **confession & kiss** in Ch12–13.\n\n- **Public demo (Ch14)**  \n  - Driven by: their “language should scale” argument in Forge Night (Slot A) + Ch8–10.  \n  - It’s not “the Council asked them;” it’s “they talked themselves into making this public.”\n\nThat’s how dialogue becomes a **cause**, not just commentary.  \nConversation → internal shift → external decision → consequence → new conversation → repeat.\n\n---\n\nIf you want, next step we can do is:  \nPick one chapter (say, Ch9 or Ch11) and literally mark: **this line of dialogue → this later choice** so you’ve got a visible chain. But structurally, the breakdown above is how the whole novella hangs together with character and conversation actually driving the machine."
          ]
        },

    "d2075a36-ad5d-4f21-a9c3-ef818a1cf78e": {

    "d258d98a-6891-4a6e-92d2-c948bccb8cd8": {

      ],

      "message": {

        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# Retry inspection without the bad import\r\nfrom pathlib import Path\r\nimport re\r\n\r\np = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds-REV-InterludeI-Corlexi.md\")\r\nassert p.exists(), \"MD file missing\"\r\ntext = p.read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\nprint(text[:2000])\r\n\r\n# Find all lines that look like CHAPTER headings\r\nheads = re.findall(r\"^CHAPTER\\s+.*\", text, flags=re.MULTILINE)\r\nprint(\"\\nFound CHAPTER-like headings:\", len(heads))\r\nfor h in heads[:12]:\r\n    print(h)"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1763206440.157838,
        "end_turn": false,
        "id": "d258d98a-6891-4a6e-92d2-c948bccb8cd8",
        "metadata": {
          "can_save": false,

    "d389a2ee-ef7f-4d73-8793-eca664944f56": {
      "children": [
        "184c86f4-00f3-4a4d-8d7b-b785bc96865a"
      ],
      "id": "d389a2ee-ef7f-4d73-8793-eca664944f56",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# Apply Pass 1 trims to Chapters 1–3 using robust chapter extraction\r\nfrom pathlib import Path\r\nimport re\r\nfrom docx import Document\r\n\r\nsrc = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds-REV-InterludeI-Corlexi.md\")\r\ntext = src.read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\n\r\ndef get_span(n, content):\r\n    m = re.search(rf\"^Chapter\\s+{n}:\\s.*?(?=^Chapter\\s+\\d+:|\\Z)\", content, flags=re.MULTILINE|re.DOTALL)\r\n    return m\r\n\r\ndef apply_replacements(block, reps, change_log):\r\n    before = block\r\n    for label, pat, repl, flags in reps:\r\n        new_block, n = re.subn(pat, repl, block, flags=flags)\r\n        if n:\r\n            change_log.append(f\"- [{label}] {n}×: `{pat}` → `{repl}`\")\r\n            block = new_block\r\n    return before, block\r\n\r\nchange_log = []\r\n\r\n# Chapter 1\r\nm1 = get_span(1, text); assert m1\r\nstart1, end1 = m1.span()\r\nch1 = text[start1:end1]\r\n\r\nreps1 = [\r\n    # Remove copper taste + pennies meta aside where it appears on a single line\r\n    (\"Ch1 copper/pennies removal (line)\",\r\n     r\"“?It tastes wrong,” Jhace said\\. “?Like old pennies\\.” Then laughed at himself because pennies weren’t a thing here, just a story someone’s grandfather had told about some other city, some other casting\\.\", \r\n     r\"\", re.DOTALL),\r\n    # Fallback: remove any line containing 'Like old pennies.'\r\n    (\"Ch1 stray pennies fallback\", r\".*Like old pennies\\..*\\n?\", r\"\", re.MULTILINE),\r\n    # Tighten Underface/bone\r\n    (\"Ch1 Underface bone trim\",\r\n     r\"The Underface carried the city’s heat the way bone bears weight: mostly without complaint, until it suddenly complained too much\\.\", \r\n     r\"The Underface carried the city’s heat the way bone bears weight: mostly without complaint.\", re.DOTALL),\r\n]\r\nch1_before, ch1_after = apply_replacements(ch1, reps1, change_log)\r\ntext = text[:start1] + ch1_after + text[end1:]\r\n\r\n# Chapter 2\r\nm2 = get_span(2, text); assert m2\r\nstart2, end2 = m2.span()\r\nch2 = text[start2:end2]\r\n\r\nreps2 = [\r\n    (\"Ch2 remove pretty/true aphorism\", r\"Pretty rarely corresponded to true\\.\\s*\", r\"\", re.DOTALL),\r\n]\r\nch2_before, ch2_after = apply_replacements(ch2, reps2, change_log)\r\ntext = text[:start2] + ch2_after + text[end2:]\r\n\r\n# Chapter 3\r\nm3 = get_span(3, text); assert m3\r\nstart3, end3 = m3.span()\r\nch3 = text[start3:end3]\r\n\r\nreps3 = [\r\n    (\"Ch3 remove 'He ran.' lines\", r\"(?m)^\\s*He ran\\.\\s*$\", r\"\", re.MULTILINE),\r\n    (\"Ch3 trim indulgent aside\", r\"\\s*Humans loved to feel foolish in exchange for not dying\\.\", r\"\", re.DOTALL),\r\n    (\"Ch3 remove 'almost pretty' line\", r\"\\s*It was almost pretty if you pretended you weren’t in the middle of it\\.\", r\"\", re.DOTALL),\r\n]\r\nch3_before, ch3_after = apply_replacements(ch3, reps3, change_log)\r\ntext = text[:start3] + ch3_after + text[end3:]\r\n\r\n# Write outputs\r\nout_md = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds-PASS1-Trim-Ch1-3.md\")\r\nout_md.write_text(text, encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\n\r\n# Make DOCX\r\ndoc = Document()\r\nfor line in text.split(\"\\n\"):\r\n    doc.add_paragraph(line)\r\nout_docx = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds-PASS1-Trim-Ch1-3.docx\")\r\ndoc.save(str(out_docx))\r\n\r\n# Change log\r\nlog = [\"# Pass 1 Trims — Chapters 1–3\",\r\n       \"## Changes Applied\",\r\n       *change_log,\r\n       \"## Excerpts (Before → After)\"]\r\ndef excerpt(label, before, after):\r\n    return f\"\\n### {label}\\n\\n**Before (first 500 chars):**\\n\\n{before[:500]}\\n\\n**After (first 500 chars):**\\n\\n{after[:500]}\\n\"\r\n\r\nlog.append(excerpt(\"Chapter 1\", ch1_before, ch1_after))\r\nlog.append(excerpt(\"Chapter 2\", ch2_before, ch2_after))\r\nlog.append(excerpt(\"Chapter 3\", ch3_before, ch3_after))\r\n\r\nlog_path = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Pass1-Trims-Ch1-3-Log.md\")\r\nlog_path.write_text(\"\\n\".join(log), encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\n\r\nprint(\"MD:\", out_md)\r\nprint(\"DOCX:\", out_docx)\r\nprint(\"Log:\", log_path)"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1763206463.114231,
        "end_turn": false,

    "d4e8106c-aadd-4614-97b9-8b0bbe51368d": {
      "children": [
        "5c82da22-03b2-4f93-ae5f-c8bdc2961102"
      ],
      "id": "d4e8106c-aadd-4614-97b9-8b0bbe51368d",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "Light pooled in the glass like water that had forgotten how to fall. Tiffani set the sighting frame against the east pane and brought the horizon to a hair-true line across the crosshairs. The city below held steady—riser smoke, a few early carts, the Outer Crown throwing its ribs against the pale sky. The line did not hold. It bowed, then eased back, as if the world’s edge had surface tension and a thumb pressed it from the other side.\n\nShe blinked. The distortion stayed.\n\n“Frame two,” she said.\n\nMikel—junior, eager, quiet when reminded—passed the second frame without comment. He had learned not to ask what she saw until she told him how to see it. Tiffani braced the frame’s heel against the sill and adjusted the angle by half a finger, then another half, until the wire grid and the distant roofline came into clean coincidence. She held three breaths, counted a set, then checked the eastern rim again. Bow. Ease. A slow, uncommitted fracture of straightness.\n\nThe glass-house was all panes and ribs and clean walkway. No benches. No glory-hole heat. Just sightlines, measurement, records. Her kind of room. She noted the deviation in the ledger: “Watch 1, pre-Shaping. East horizon: concave deflection, one finger drop at two bars measured elevation. Repeatable.” She made a second mark: an underscore for uncertainty. Protocol.\n\n“Humidity,” Mikel offered, because he could not help himself.\n\n“Humidity makes the air a lens,” she said. “This is a hand too strong for humidity.”\n\nHe nodded like he understood and did not. That was fine. He would learn by standing beside, by arranging tools the way she asked, by watching her move and not moving wrong. Precision required a quiet field. People rippled the air.\n\n“Frame three,” she said.\n\nHe crossed the walkway quick as a trot and brought the angle-cage with the measuring cords wound tidy. She approved of that, silently. The cage was a spider of brass and string: eight spokes, each with a knot at a span, and a sliding bead for fine adjustment. She set it in the window’s corner where the mullions met at scribe-true ninety. The cage gave her four sightlines: cardinal compass, the city’s own plumb. She aligned the cords to the ribs of the Crown and to the far heat-rib that walked toward the edge of sight, then sighted through each open space in turn.\n\nFirst sight: clean. Second: clean. Third: a shimmer that could have been heat above a quench pool if the day were hotter than breath-steam. Fourth: the same bowing line. The same reluctant return.\n\nShe wrote again. “Repeat deflection. Likely systematic.”\n\n“Systematic means we can solve it,” Mikel said, too hopeful.\n\n“Systematic means it is real,” she said. “Solving comes after knowing.”\n\nHe bit his lip, chastened. He was not wrong. He just liked the after better than the knowing. Most people did.\n\nTiffani switched windows. North had always been her favorite: clear line from the glass-house to the low slope and out across nearly two reaches of flat land before the ground rose again into the distant loom of other cities. Today, the north horizon bulged outward a finger and a half at the same elevation, as if the world’s pane had a bubble trapped inside it.\n\nShe swapped the cage for the plumb-sight—two bars of straight-edge with a drop-weight on a fine line, plumb-true when the weight stilled. She steadied her breathing. The weight steadied with her. The horizon did not. It lensed, bowed, settled, lensed again.\n\nA watch passed in work: four measurements in each direction, cords realigned between, panes wiped to avoid refraction from smudges, every number entered twice, once bold, once light. She stitched the data in her head as she wrote, mapping deflection like a crack propagating through a sheet: invisible at first, then everywhere once you taught your eye its shape. East and north bowed inward. South and west had an outward bulge that did not match wind or heat. The pattern was not pretty. Pretty rarely corresponded to true.\n\n“Do you want tea?” Mikel asked at last.\n\n“I want the world to stop bending,” she said, without heat. “Tea will not oblige it.”\n\nHe flushed and moved away, making himself useful with a rag on the panes. His reflection crossed the glass like a ghost. She did not look at him. She watched the distance.\n\n“This is not local,” she said after a long set, mostly to the ledger. “Not a furnace plume, not a vent. The curvature shifts with angle but remains centered on… not us.”\n\n“Could be a pressure change,” Mikel said, still hopeful, because pressure you could blame on something with a valve.\n\n“Then the birds would fly closer to ground,” she said, pointing with the pen where three specks cut their arcs clean. “They don’t.”\n\n“Oh.”\n\nHe was very young, and not. People who came to the glass learned to become older than their bodies would claim. Tiffani had been twenty when Marisse took her hand and made her trace, with one finger, the exact line between almost and true. She could still feel the humiliation when she went a hair wide. She could still feel—no. She folded the memory back and slid it into its proper slot. What the mind would widen to pain, the work pared away.\n\nMikel hovered. “Master Merrow?”\n\n“Do not call me that,” she said, easier than it felt. “Tiffani is sufficient.”\n\n“Yes. Tiffani.” He hesitated. “Why do you work alone up here?”\n\n“Fewer bodies to warm the air,” she said, eyes still on the horizon.\n\nHe waited.\n\nShe sighed. Precision was not always a numbers problem. Sometimes it was a tolerance you set around words. “Focus is a finite resource. Noise is a thief. People are—” She picked the line up by its clean edge, held it a breath, and set it down. “Imprecise.”\n\nHe nodded like a student marking a note he would misread later. She wanted to tell him the truer thing—people leave, numbers don’t—but that belonged in the margin of a different ledger. He would not learn it from her saying so. He would measure it on his own.\n\nShe turned the east frame a half-hand to the left, then another half-hand, bringing a far tower into line with the frame’s upright. The tower wavered. Not shimmering, not the dance of heat. A slow, deliberate quiver as if someone had taken the whole bar of distance and twisted it a quarter-turn.\n\nTiffani’s neck prickled. The glass itself responded: a faint tick in the panes as the mullions sighed, a hairline song she felt through her fingertips on the sill. The sound did not carry; it pressed. The ledger’s ink line trembled as she wrote. The weight on the plumb-sight described a wider circle before it settled again.\n\nMikel went still in the way of animals.\n\n“What is it?” he whispered.\n\n“Something large,” she said. “Not here. Not far.”\n\nHe looked at her reflection. “Is that useful?”\n\n“It tells us whether to stand on the floor or under a brace,” she said. “Stand under a brace.”\n\nHe moved without argument to the rib where the glass-house’s bones crossed, and set his back against it. Tiffani kept her post at the window. The correct place to measure was not the safe place. Choosing otherwise gave you exquisite precision on the wrong thing.\n\nThe bowing line deepened. The deflection now matched a hand at two bars elevation—no, more, a hand and three fingers. The change outpaced the end of her last sentence. She started another and found there was no place to anchor it. The world’s skin went flexible and kept going.\n\n“Tell me where our closest proof-wall sits,” she said, mind already mapping stresses as if she could brace the horizon with a handful of glass struts.\n\n“Between the Outer Crown and the Foundry Run,” Mikel said, repeating lesson. “Two bars south of us. Why?”\n\n“If it fails, the lower districts will take the surge,” she said. “Look down. Look for pressure you can see.”\n\nHe edged to the window beside her. The city held, for now. Plumes rose. Cart wheels traced their lines. People moved at a walk; a few at a run. No one screamed. The world did not announce a catastrophe before it landed. That would be courteous. The world was not courteous.\n\nThe sound arrived as a pressure first. Not noise—a change in center. The panes took it, and the iron ribs passed it into the floor. Tiffani’s jaw ached. She swallowed and felt the swallow bounce.\n\nThen, past the north horizon, the air found its voice. A low roar, too large to fit in the ear, rolled toward them as if the world had begun to inhale and forgot where to stop. Birds broke their arcs midwing, recalculated, fled. The tower she had used for sighting now leaned, a finger only, then a hand, not by itself, but because the line between it and everything else had shortened.\n\nMikel cursed—short, honest. “What do we do?”\n\n“We measure,” she said, because answer and compulsion were the same. She set the frame for one more reading and kept her hand steady while the line bent. The deflection no longer crept; it surged. Her scribe-true hand could not track every rise. She marked center, edge, and returned. Three points define a circle. Triangulate the impossible and it becomes, at least, named.\n\nThe roar thickened until the glass-house sang at it, ribs humming in sympathy. Tiffani fixed the crosshairs on the bulge where the horizon had stopped pretending to be straight and said, to the ledger, to herself, to the future: “Final reading: horizon no longer a line.”\n\nThe floor shivered. Somewhere below, a brace cried like a struck bell. Mikel grabbed the rib and held on.\n\nTiffani closed the ledger, set it under the sill, and rested both hands on the cool pane. Hairline songs ran under her palms, crossing, adding, canceling: interference made audible. She could map the fractures of sound even if she could not stop them. Precision in the face of force was not nothing. It was a promise to be ready when the world finished inhaling and whatever came next hit.\n\nThe roar kept coming. The glass pressed back. And the horizon—no longer a horizon—bulged outward as if the planet itself had been pierced from above and the pressure wave was the answer."
          ]
        },

    "d65c8423-8e4c-43fe-91fa-ec6a5590d893": {
      "children": [
        "bdcc5ace-c8a7-4248-a070-aa23906e884b"
      ],
      "id": "d65c8423-8e4c-43fe-91fa-ec6a5590d893",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "ani relationship is **rock-solid partnership** by N02 start\n- First kiss happened (Ch12), love declared (Ch14), partnership proven (Ch15)\n- N02 escalates external pressure (factions) while deepening intimacy\n- Bram's death (N02) will test partnership severely\n- Chloen's death (N02) will traumatize bothâ€”first major shared grief\n\n---\n\n## SECTION 10: WRITER'S VOICE NOTES\n\n### Prose Style (N01)\n\n**Jhace Chapters:**\n- Sensory-forward (smell of iron, chalk dust, warmth)\n- Short sentences during action\n- Internal monologue = practical, immediate\n- Metaphors from craft (metal remembers, bolts hold true)\n\n**Tiffani Chapters:**\n- Geometric/precise language\n- Longer sentences with careful structure\n- Internal monologue = analytical, measured\n- Metaphors from measurement (lines, angles, balance)\n\n**Interludes:**\n- Ominous, distant\n- Obsessive focus on single idea\n- Cost accumulation visceral\n- Foreshadowing without explaining\n\n### Relationship Writing\n\n**Early (Ch1-5):**\n- Formal language, professional distance\n- \"Jhace\" and \"Tiffani\" (full names)\n- Touch rare, accidental\n- Communication through craft\n\n**Middle (Ch6-10):**\n- Relaxing into familiarity\n- Names soften (still formal but warmer)\n- Touch intentional but careful\n- Communication verbal + craft\n\n**Late (Ch11-15):**\n- Intimate shorthand\n- Sometimes just \"him\" / \"her\" when POV clear\n- Touch constant, necessary\n- Communication mostly nonverbal\n\n### Magic Writing\n\n**NEVER:**\n- Explain mechanics theoretically\n- Use RPG-style power descriptions\n- Make it feel effortless\n- Forget cost\n\n**ALWAYS:**\n- Show through physical sensation\n-\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1763136190.3658,
        "end_turn": null,

    "d8d30193-7fa8-45be-bd59-d0811d19c3c9": {
      "children": [
        "60f3f4d7-ecc2-4dab-80d7-0e33af6bc1ba"
      ],
      "id": "d8d30193-7fa8-45be-bd59-d0811d19c3c9",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "user"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# **N01 REVISION PLAN**\n\n**Total Time**: 8-12 hours  \n**Goal**: Tighten prose density, advance vein-burn stakes, give Chloen agency, add sensory grounding\n\n***\n\n## **PASS 1: PROSE TRIM** (4-6 hours)\n\n### **Target Chapters**: 1-10 (leave 11-15 untouched)\n\n### **What to Cut**:\n\n**1. Stacked Metaphors**\n- Find sentences with 3+ images competing for attention\n- Pick the strongest image, delete the rest\n- Example: \"The hum threw a stutter through his skull that made his teeth hurt\" → \"The hum made his teeth ache\"\n\n**2. Redundant Similes** (cut 50%)\n- Keep metaphors that do structural/characterization work\n- Delete decorative comparisons\n- Ask: Does this image tell me something new about character or world? If no, cut it.\n\n**3. Over-Modified Nouns**\n- Find nouns with 2-3 adjectives\n- Keep one, delete the rest\n- Example: \"thin, pale, chalk-dusted light\" → \"chalk-dusted light\"\n\n**4. Sensation Stacking**\n- Find moments where multiple sensory details compete\n- Keep the most specific one\n- Example: \"He tasted limestone and felt the grit and smelled old heat\" → \"He tasted limestone dust\"\n\n### **How to Execute**:\n1. Read Ch1 aloud, marking any sentence that feels labored\n2. For each marked sentence, circle all metaphors/similes\n3. Pick the best one, delete the rest\n4. Repeat for Ch2-10\n5. Aim to reduce ~100 words per chapter (~1,000 words total)\n\n***\n\n## **PASS 2: VEIN-BURN PROGRESSION** (1 hour)\n\n### **Problem**: After Ch11, the copper-line cost stops advancing visibly.\n\n### **Fix**: Add 1 sentence per chapter tracking vein-burn growth.\n\n### **Specific Additions**:\n\n**Chapter 12** (after restraint training scene, before sleep):\n```\nHe checked the line before sleep. Four centimeters now—halfway to his hand. \nThe discipline had cost him half a finger's width in a single day.\n```\n\n**Chapter 13** (morning check, after waking):\n```\nThe copper-brown line sat at four centimeters, patient as law. It hadn't moved \nsince yesterday. For now, stopping held.\n```\n\n**Chapter 14** (after public demonstration, checking wrist):\n```\nHe rolled his wrist and checked truth. Four and a quarter—the demonstration \nhad taken more than he'd wanted to spend. He pulled the sleeve down and didn't \nmake bargains with himself.\n```\n\n**Chapter 15** (final check at forge):\n```\nThe line had stopped at four and a quarter centimeters. Not longer, but the \ncopper-gold warmth in his chest sat thin as morning mist. He had coins left. \nNot many.\n```\n\n### **Where to Insert**:\n- Ch12: Near end, before \"When the bell lied...\" closing\n- Ch13: Early, after Jhace wakes/checks himself\n- Ch14: After demonstration sequence, during breath-check moment\n- Ch15: Near end, when he's at forge checking tools\n\n***\n\n## **PASS 3: CHLOEN AGENCY** (1-2 hours)\n\n### **Problem**: Chloen observes events but doesn't change outcomes.\n\n### **Fix**: Give her two plot-impacting actions.\n\n### **Addition 1: Chapter 9** (Manual Creation Scene)\n\n**Find**: The scene where they're creating the field manual  \n**Add after manual discussion**:\n```\nChloen took the chalk from his hand. \"I'm better at pictures,\" she said, \nand proved it. Her drawings were clumsy but true—the kind of true that \nmade men remember the mark when the page was gone. By late watch, she'd \ndrawn three pages that looked like they'd been torn from the city's own ledger.\n```\n\n### **Addition 2: Chapter 14** (Public Demonstration)\n\n**Find**: The crowd scene during demonstration  \n**Add during or after demonstration**:\n```\nAt the crowd's edge, Chloen moved between clusters with a bundle of hand-copied \nmanuals. Her drawings—rough but honest—spread through hands faster than speech. \nBy late glow, three crews had manuals they'd copied themselves, her pictures \nmultiplying like chalk marks on a hot grid.\n```\n\n***\n\n## **PASS 4: SENSORY GROUNDING** (1-2 hours)\n\n### **Problem**: Some scenes feel abstract; readers lose physical orientation.\n\n### **Fix**: Add 1 grounding sentence per major scene (Ch1-10).\n\n### **What to Add**:\nOne sentence establishing temperature, smell, or light quality at scene start.\n\n### **Examples by Chapter**:\n\n**Ch1** (opening scene at Underface):\n```\nThe Underface smelled like limestone and old sweat.\n```\n\n**Ch2** (Tiffani measuring horizon):\n```\nHeat made the measurement frame slick under her palms.\n```\n\n**Ch3** (purge sequence):\n```\nHeat from the purge made the air shimmer like glass warping.\n```\n\n**Ch4** (glass work):\n```\nThe crucible's heat pushed against his face like a flat palm.\n```\n\n**Ch5** (first SoulPulse):\n```\nCopper-gold warmth flooded his chest, then burned.\n```\n\n**Ch6** (teaching begins):\n```\nChalk dust made the air taste like dry bone.\n```\n\n**Ch7** (synchronized work):\n```\nTheir breath made small clouds in the morning cold.\n```\n\n**Ch8** (crowd learning):\n```\nTwo hundred bodies made the rib warmer than the bells said it should be.\n```\n\n**Ch9** (manual creation):\n```\nInk stained his fingers the color of old bruises.\n```\n\n**Ch10** (vulnerability confessions):\n```\nNight-shift cold made his scars ache.\n```\n\n### **How to Execute**:\n1. Read each chapter start\n2. Ask: Can I feel temperature, smell, or light quality?\n3. If no, add one sentence establishing physical environment\n4. Place near scene opening (within first 3 paragraphs)\n\n***\n\n## **CHECKLIST**\n\n### **Pass 1: Prose Trim**\n- [ ] Ch1: Cut stacked metaphors, redundant similes (aim -100 words)\n- [ ] Ch2: Cut stacked metaphors, redundant similes (aim -100 words)\n- [ ] Ch3: Cut stacked metaphors, redundant similes (aim -100 words)\n- [ ] Ch4: Cut stacked metaphors, redundant similes (aim -100 words)\n- [ ] Ch5: Cut stacked metaphors, redundant similes (aim -100 words)\n- [ ] Ch6: Cut stacked metaphors, redundant similes (aim -100 words)\n- [ ] Ch7: Cut stacked metaphors, redundant similes (aim -100 words)\n- [ ] Ch8: Cut stacked metaphors, redundant similes (aim -100 words)\n- [ ] Ch9: Cut stacked metaphors, redundant similes (aim -100 words)\n- [ ] Ch10: Cut stacked metaphors, redundant similes (aim -100 words)\n\n### **Pass 2: Vein-Burn Progression**\n- [ ] Ch12: Add vein-burn check (4cm, halfway to hand)\n- [ ] Ch13: Add vein-burn check (still 4cm, stopping holds)\n- [ ] Ch14: Add vein-burn check (4.25cm, demo cost visible)\n- [ ] Ch15: Add vein-burn check (still 4.25cm, warmth depleted)\n\n### **Pass 3: Chloen Agency**\n- [ ] Ch9: Add Chloen drawing manual pages\n- [ ] Ch14: Add Chloen distributing hand-copied manuals in crowd\n\n### **Pass 4: Sensory Grounding**\n- [ ] Ch1: Add temperature/smell/light sentence\n- [ ] Ch2: Add temperature/smell/light sentence\n- [ ] Ch3: Add temperature/smell/light sentence\n- [ ] Ch4: Add temperature/smell/light sentence\n- [ ] Ch5: Add temperature/smell/light sentence\n- [ ] Ch6: Add temperature/smell/light sentence\n- [ ] Ch7: Add temperature/smell/light sentence\n- [ ] Ch8: Add temperature/smell/light sentence\n- [ ] Ch9: Add temperature/smell/light sentence\n- [ ] Ch10: Add temperature/smell/light sentence\n\n***\n\n## **WORKFLOW**\n\n**Session 1** (2-3 hours): Prose trim, Ch1-5  \n**Session 2** (2-3 hours): Prose trim, Ch6-10  \n**Session 3** (1 hour): Add vein-burn progression (Ch12-15)  \n**Session 4** (1 hour): Add Chloen agency scenes (Ch9, Ch14)  \n**Session 5** (1-2 hours): Add sensory grounding (Ch1-10)\n\n***\n\n## **FINAL RESULT**\n\n**Word Count**: ~25,500 (down from 26,500)  \n**Prose Density**: Balanced (peaks soar, valleys breathe)  \n**Stakes**: Escalating (vein-burn visibly advances Ch12-15)  \n**Character Agency**: Chloen co-creates system  \n**Physical Grounding**: Readers can feel temperature/smell/light\n\n**After completion**: Ready for professional copyedit."
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1763195022.008356,
        "end_turn": null,

    "e2878b99-464b-4bf3-8fd3-e6953a9ac656": {
      "children": [
        "f85e7d96-0665-490c-8355-a28b33747c8e"
      ],
      "id": "e2878b99-464b-4bf3-8fd3-e6953a9ac656",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "Updated manuscript: /mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds-PASS5-InterludeI-Fixed.md\nDOCX: /mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds-PASS5-InterludeI-Fixed.docx\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1763207578.1552384,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "e2878b99-464b-4bf3-8fd3-e6953a9ac656",
        "metadata": {
          "ada_visualizations": [
            {
              "file_id": "file-KFWRuncZVaMdV1W4mFXJ1b",
              "title": "Forbidden-term scan results (Pass 5)",
              "type": "table"
            }
          ],
          "aggregate_result": {
            "code": "# Retry with more robust locating: find the Interlude I region; if missing, insert after CHAPTER 5 and before CHAPTER 6.\r\n\r\nfrom pathlib import Path\r\nimport re\r\nfrom docx import Document\r\n\r\nsrc = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds-PASS2-3-4.md\")\r\ntext = src.read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\n\r\nnew_interlude = \"\"\"INTERLUDE I: PRE-GOD STIRRINGS\r\n\r\nPART A: CORLEXI — PERFECT LINES\r\n\r\nPrism was all angles pretending to be virtue. Corlexi loved it for that lie.\r\n\r\nHe stood on a ribbed gallery high in the half-built Spire of Measures, wind combed thin through the lattice, apprentices scattered below like commas in a sentence that would not end. Chalk lines webbed the floor. Plumb-threads hung perfectly still—not because the air was calm, but because he refused motion.\r\n\r\n“Again,” he said.\r\n\r\nThey lifted the truss and set it into the mouth of the waiting frame. Blue-silver threaded his fingers and stayed, a constant wire under the skin. He pressed it into the join—not hard, not soft, exactly—until the two faces of steel forgot their seam. The Spire’s hum shifted half a note closer to satisfied.\r\n\r\n“Master,” Adren ventured, careful. “We’ve been at it three watches.”\r\n\r\n“We are not tired,” Corlexi said, and made the statement feel true the way a line becomes true when enough force is applied. The boy straightened. Everyone did.\r\n\r\nA brace complained far below—soft, then quiet, chastened by a glance Corlexi did not bother to send. The city beyond lay sharpened by late glow, every roofline cutting the sky. He loved the certainty of edges. Loved the moment when a bracket’s petty war with gravity ended and the bracket conceded he had always been right. Disaster had a geometry; it could be designed against. If he perfected the forms, the world would remember how to hold itself.\r\n\r\nThey shifted the west run. Adren’s boot slipped on chalk and oil. He snatched at air, found nothing, tilted. Meve lunged, caught a sleeve, lost purchase. Corlexi moved a hand-width—no farther—caught Adren’s collar, kept the line of the truss with his other hand as if both tasks were one equation. Blue-silver whispered at his knuckles where they touched steel. The apprentice hung a breath over the drop, feet bicycling emptily.\r\n\r\n“Still,” Corlexi said, as if calming a beam. Adren froze. Corlexi set the boy upright without permitting the truss to skew a single finger off true. The metal sang compliance. He released the collar last.\r\n\r\n“Thank you, Master,” Adren said, scarce air and shame in it.\r\n\r\n“Do not thank me for maintaining form,” Corlexi said. “Thank the form for permitting you to remain inside it.”\r\n\r\nThey worked on. The glow that had begun as event sat now like background, like breath. A pleasant pressure gathered in Corlexi’s knuckles; his fingers did not want to fold. He did not require them to. He shaped force through flat hands and let his wrists carry the line. Hands that lacked discipline shook; structures did not. He preferred structures.\r\n\r\nMeve squinted along the north run. “A hair over-stiff there. If wind takes a mood, we’ll get chatter.”\r\n\r\n“Remove the hair,” Corlexi said, “after we seat this.”\r\n\r\nBlue-silver settled into the truss like a clean number. The Spire answered with a long, low approval. Corlexi let himself breathe.\r\n\r\nHe imagined a city where every rib and brace and gutter held to this logic. Where bulges in the sky—whatever they were—could find no purchase because there were no irregularities to amplify. Where people lived inside certainty and forgot the taste of fear.\r\n\r\nHis thumb would not touch his forefinger. He watched the failure to meet with mild curiosity, as if it belonged to some other man left on his scaffold by mistake. He tried the other hand. Same result. Stiffness; bright, clean pain when he insisted. A ledger line, not a warning.\r\n\r\n“Water,” he said at last, magnanimous. “A swallow. Then the west truss.”\r\n\r\nRelief moved across the crew like wind over grass. He let it. They drank. They breathed. The blue-silver under his skin did not dim.\r\n\r\nPerfection requires sacrifice. He had always been willing to pay.\r\n\r\nPART B: MOBEL — CLEANSING FIRE\r\n\r\nThe desert at Heattide stripped the polite off things. Mobel liked the honesty.\r\n\r\nHis tent was a shadow pinned to hard ground, canvas singing under a sun that refused to age. The woman on the pallet had a wound along the thigh that smelled like rot turned argumentative. Her husband knelt beside her with hope stretched thin between both hands, the way men hold rope when the beam is already failing.\r\n\r\nMobel washed his fingers in water he could have drunk and did not. He wiped them dry and worked with what he had: boiled cloth, clean band, a narrow iron warmed in coals until it went the color of admission. Heat has a voice; it speaks of endings. He let it speak through his hand.\r\n\r\n“Hold her,” he said.\r\n\r\nThe woman screamed once, clean and strong. Flesh bubbled, then flattened. The stink lifted and left something metallic and sharp behind. He walked the wound’s edge with care, burning away anything that argued with health. “Contamination requires fire,” Mobel said, as he said for all, a simple litany to steady hands. “Clean is the law.”\r\n\r\nOutside the tent, a knot of watchers held their breath in a single borrowed chest. They had followed him here—quiet women with hard faces; men with scars and eyes that had stopped searching for soft places; two youths too young to be certain and hungry for someone who seemed to be. They liked the clarity he offered. He liked that they liked it. It made the work simpler.\r\n\r\nHe bound the wound in boiled cloth. The woman sobbed into the cool and then, exhausted, slept. Her husband reached to kiss Mobel’s hands. Mobel lifted them out of reach. The skin had swollen from the iron—a pressure under the knuckles like water seeking a seam. He flexed and felt the complaint spread along the tendons like a fault line mapping itself. He filed it as inventory. Costs were comfort when counted correctly.\r\n\r\nA second couple pushed forward. The man’s forearm was banded with ink that crawled into an old cut. The skin around it went the wrong colors. “Please,” the woman said, throat hoarse. “We walked a reach.”\r\n\r\nMobel looked at the arm, then at the ink. “You kept filth in the skin,” he said, not loud. “You chose it.”\r\n\r\n“It’s my clan,” the man rasped. “Not filth.”\r\n\r\n“Then let your clan heal you,” Mobel said, and stepped aside. The watchers murmured—approval more than anger, relief at a rule applied. The woman begged again, soft, the way people do when their words have no leverage left.\r\n\r\nMobel did not compete with the desert. The desert corrected everything eventually.\r\n\r\nHe stepped into the sun because he liked the way heat made the ache in his hands feel less like pain and more like law. Wind picked up; the tent answered with a different song, one that had learned the low third note threading the world since the sky had bulged and passed. Mobel ignored it. His note was simpler: clean or not.\r\n\r\nHe looked east, where the horizon cut pure across sand, and thought of cities with their compromises. Thought of the way rot hid in corners and called itself neighborly. He rested swollen hands against the tent pole until the ache made a proper accounting.\r\n\r\n“Only the clean will stand,” he said. Words moved through the watchers like a heat-front, leaving belief behind in their wake.\r\n\r\nThen he went back in to boil more cloth, to pour more water he would not drink, to answer one more body with the fire it deserved.\r\n\"\"\"\r\n\r\n# Try to locate an existing Interlude I header\r\nm = re.search(r'(?im)^INTERLUDE\\s*I\\b.*$', text)\r\nif m:\r\n    start = m.start()\r\n    # Find next chapter header\r\n    n = re.search(r'(?im)^CHAPTER\\s+\\d+\\b.*$', text[m.end():])\r\n    if n:\r\n        end = m.end() + n.start()\r\n    else:\r\n        end = len(text)\r\n    updated = text[:start] + new_interlude + \"\\n\\n\" + text[end:]\r\nelse:\r\n    # No interlude found; insert after CHAPTER 5 and before CHAPTER 6\r\n    # Find CHAPTER 5 header\r\n    c5 = re.search(r'(?im)^CHAPTER\\s*5\\b.*$', text)\r\n    c6 = re.search(r'(?im)^CHAPTER\\s*6\\b.*$', text)\r\n    if c5 and c6 and c6.start() > c5.start():\r\n        insert_at = c6.start()\r\n        updated = text[:insert_at] + \"\\n\\n\" + new_interlude + \"\\n\\n\" + text[insert_at:]\r\n    else:\r\n        # As a fallback, append to end\r\n        updated = text.rstrip() + \"\\n\\n\" + new_interlude + \"\\n\"\r\n\r\n# Forbidden-term scan\r\nforbidden = [\"white-gold\", \"Purity\", \"Entropy\"]\r\nhits = {term: [] for term in forbidden}\r\nfor term in forbidden:\r\n    for mm in re.finditer(term, updated, flags=re.IGNORECASE):\r\n        s = max(0, mm.start()-40); e = min(len(updated), mm.end()+40)\r\n        hits[term].append(updated[s:e])\r\n\r\n# Save files\r\nout_md = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds-PASS5-InterludeI-Fixed.md\")\r\nout_md.write_text(updated, encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\n\r\ndoc = Document()\r\nfor line in updated.split(\"\\n\"):\r\n    doc.add_paragraph(line)\r\nout_docx = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01-Grip-That-Holds-PASS5-InterludeI-Fixed.docx\")\r\ndoc.save(str(out_docx))\r\n\r\n# Report\r\nfrom caas_jupyter_tools import display_dataframe_to_user\r\nimport pandas as pd\r\n\r\nrows = []\r\nfor term, snips in hits.items():\r\n    for sn in snips:\r\n        rows.append({\"term\": term, \"context\": sn})\r\ndf = pd.DataFrame(rows) if rows else pd.DataFrame(columns=[\"term\",\"context\"])\r\ndisplay_dataframe_to_user(\"Forbidden-term scan results (Pass 5)\", df)\r\n\r\nprint(\"Updated manuscript:\", out_md)\r\nprint(\"DOCX:\", out_docx)",
